name: Pepper X
description: **Pepper X** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super-hot chili pepper** developed by **Ed Currie** of the *PuckerButt Pepper Company* in South Carolina, USA. It averages around **2.69 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it the **world’s hottest certified pepper**. The fruit is **wrinkled, elongated, and green-yellow to mustard-yellow**, used mainly for **extreme hot sauces** rather than casual consumption ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pepper_X?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Pepper X”** began as a **placeholder** during development, used while legal and trademark issues were pending. Currie chose the **“X”** to symbolize both the **unknown** and **extreme** qualities of this pepper ([Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/spicy/comments/179hz0i?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). It was created by **crossbreeding the Carolina Reaper** with another ultra-hot, undisclosed variety over nearly a decade, aiming to break heat records. The name conveys **mystery, danger, and innovation** in modern chili breeding ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pepper_X?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Carolina Reaper
description: **Carolina Reaper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **extreme super-hot chili pepper** developed by **Ed Currie** of the *PuckerButt Pepper Company* in South Carolina. It averages **1.64 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, with peaks exceeding **2.2 million SHU**, making it one of the **world’s hottest peppers**. The fruit is **small, red, and bumpy**, often with a scorpion-like tail, and is prized for its **fruity flavor beneath its searing heat** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carolina_Reaper?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/carolina-reaper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Carolina Reaper”** reflects both its **origin** and **lethal heat level**. “Carolina” honors its birthplace in **South Carolina**, while “Reaper” symbolizes its **intense spiciness**, suggesting it could “reap” the unprepared consumer ([Guinness World Records](https://www.guinnessworldrecords.com/world-records/hottest-chili?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Currie bred it by crossing a **Pakistani Naga pepper** with a **Red Habanero**, spending years refining its flavor and potency. The result became **a global icon of extreme heat and chili culture** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carolina_Reaper?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Trinidad Moruga Scorpion
description: **Trinidad Moruga Scorpion** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **ultra-hot chili pepper** originating from the **Moruga District in Trinidad and Tobago**. It averages around **1.2–2 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)** and has a **fruity, slightly smoky flavor** beneath its intense burn. The pepper’s **wrinkled red pods** often feature a **stinger-like tail**, inspiring its “scorpion” nickname. It’s a favorite among chili enthusiasts seeking extreme heat for sauces and competitions ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-moruga-scorpion/?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinidad_Moruga_scorpion?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Trinidad Moruga Scorpion”** combines its **place of origin—Moruga, Trinidad—with its stinger-like shape and deadly heat** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/trinidad-moruga-scorpion/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The term “Scorpion” refers to the pepper’s **tail-like tip** resembling a scorpion’s barb. Developed and identified by the **University of Trinidad and Tobago**, it once held the title of the **world’s hottest pepper** (2012). The name symbolizes **Trinidadian pride** and the pepper’s **formidable power** in the chili world ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinidad_Moruga_scorpion?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Trinidad Scorpion Chocolate
description: **Trinidad Scorpion Chocolate** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **ultra-hot chili pepper** known for its **dark brown color** and **intense heat**, averaging **1.2 to 2 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Native to **Trinidad and Tobago**, it has a **smoky, earthy flavor** with subtle sweetness beneath its fiery punch. The pods are **wrinkled and stinger-tipped**, resembling a scorpion’s tail, and are commonly used in **hot sauces, powders, and marinades** ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-chocolate/?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/trinidad-scorpion/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Trinidad Scorpion Chocolate”** combines its **geographic origin**, **distinctive stinger-like shape**, and **deep chocolate-brown hue**. “Scorpion” references the **sharp, curved tip** of the pepper, while “Chocolate” describes its **rich, dark coloration** when mature ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-chocolate/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Originating from **Trinidad’s Moruga region**, it is a natural color variant of the red Trinidad Scorpion. The name conveys both **danger and allure**, capturing its **intense heat and exotic appearance** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinidad_Moruga_scorpion?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: 7 Pot Brain Strain
description: **7 Pot Brain Strain** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super-hot pepper** originating from **Trinidad**, famous for its **gnarled, brain-like surface** and extreme heat—often exceeding **1.2 to 1.4 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It has a **fruity, slightly floral flavor** that adds both **sweetness and fiery intensity** to sauces and salsas. The pods are typically **bright red, thick-walled, and irregularly shaped**, with a heat level that rivals the world’s hottest peppers ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-brain-strain/?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/7-pot-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Brain Strain”** reflects both **its appearance and power**. “7 Pot” refers to its Trinidadian origin—locals claim one pepper can **spice seven pots of stew**. The “Brain Strain” part was coined by chili grower **David Capiello**, who selectively bred the most wrinkled pods to resemble **the folds of a human brain** ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-brain-strain/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The result is one of the **most intimidating and visually striking super-hots** in existence ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/7-pot-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Bedfordshire Super Naga
description: **Bedfordshire Super Naga** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **British-bred super-hot chili** reaching **900,000–1,120,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces **dark red, wrinkled pods** with a fruity flavor that builds into intense heat, making it one of the **hottest market-available peppers in the UK** ([Cayenne Diane](https://www.cayennediane.com/peppers/bedfordshire-super-naga/)). The plant is **robust and productive**, suitable for both commercial and home cultivation.
aditionalInfo: The name **“Bedfordshire Super Naga”** reflects its **origin and pedigree**. “Bedfordshire” denotes the **UK county** where grower Salvatore Genovese developed it, while “Super Naga” references the **potent Naga chili lineage from India** ([Groenten Nieuws](https://www.groentennieuws.nl/article/87644/tesco-verkoopt-heetste-peper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). “Super” emphasizes its **extreme heat** compared to standard Naga varieties. The name honors both its **geographic birthplace** and **high-spice heritage**, highlighting its **UK-grown super-hot status**.

name: Dragon's Breath
description: **Dragon’s Breath pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot cultivar claimed to reach around **2.48 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features small, deeply wrinkled, bright red pods with a fruity note beneath the searing heat. Originally developed in the UK, it has become a prized and cautionary specimen among chili‑enthusiasts, primarily used for novelty or extract rather than everyday cooking. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/dragons-breath/) , [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/dragons-breath-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Dragon’s Breath”** refers both to its **mythical magnitude of heat** and its geographic connection to Wales — home of the legendary **Welsh Dragon**. The pepper was developed in a collaboration between grower Mike Smith, farmer Neal Price and Nottingham Trent University, and the name honours its origins in St Asaph, Denbighshire, Wales. While the cultivar was initially intended for medicinal capsaicin research, its extraordinary heat earned it the fiery moniker that echoes both national mythology and spicy intensity. ([Live Science/Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dragon%27s_Breath_%28chili_pepper%29))

name: Dorset Naga
description: **Dorset Naga** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a super‑hot chili pepper that can reach upwards of **544,000 to over 1,221,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features wrinkled, wedge‑shaped pods that ripen from green to fiery red, and offers a **fruity aroma with intense, slow‑building heat**. Originally developed in England’s cooler climate, it remains one of the world’s most extreme peppers. ([Cayenne Diane](https://pepperscale.com/dorset-naga/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Dorset Naga” reflects both its **region of development** and its **chili lineage**. “Dorset” honours the county in England where growers Joy Michaud and Michael Michaud of Sea Spring Seeds selected the pepper from seeds of the Bangladeshi variety Naga Morich. “Naga” traces back to the original Naga chili from northeast India and Bangladesh, tying the name to its fiery heritage. Over years of selective breeding they stabilized the variety, leading to its recognition as a distinct cultivar. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/dorset-naga-chili-pepper/) , [Authority Health Mag](https://www.authorityhealthmag.com/dorset-naga-chili/) )

name: Naga Viper
description: **Naga Viper** (*Capsicum chinense × Capsicum frutescens × Capsicum chinense “Bhut Jolokia”*) is a **hybrid super-hot chili** developed in the United Kingdom. It averages around **1.3 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, with pods that are wrinkled, conical, and red at maturity. The pepper combines **fruity, smoky flavors** with extreme heat, used primarily in **hot sauces, extracts, and chili challenges** rather than culinary applications ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/naga-viper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/naga-viper-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Naga Viper”** reflects its **genetic heritage and formidable heat**. “Naga” comes from the **Bhut Jolokia (Naga) chili** of northeast India, while “Viper” emphasizes its **deadly potency and hybrid lineage**. Developed by chili breeder **Gerald Fowler in the UK**, it is a **triple hybrid** designed to combine the heat of the Naga, Trinidad Scorpion, and Bhut Jolokia. The name conveys **danger, speed, and extreme intensity**, making it instantly recognizable among super-hot chili enthusiasts ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/naga-viper-chili-peppers/)).

name: Spanish Naga
description: **Spanish Naga Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super-hot chili cultivar** rated around **1,086,844 SHU**, with compact, round-to-tapered pods that ripen from green to red and a wrinkled, knobby surface. It has a **fruity, citrus-like aroma beneath extreme heat**, mainly used in **sauces and specialty chili applications** ([Cayenne Diane](https://www.cayennediane.com/peppers/gibralta-spanish-naga-pepper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/gibralta-spanish-naga-chili-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Spanish Naga”** (also called “Gibraltar Naga”) reflects both **geography and lineage**. Although grown in **Spain**, the pepper was developed in the **UK from Indian Naga-type peppers**. “Naga” connects it to the **fiery Indian/Bangladeshi chili family**, while “Spanish” acknowledges the **Spanish cultivation environment** that intensifies heat. The name honors **its hybrid origin, extreme potency, and geographic influences** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/gibralta-spanish-naga-chili-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Bhut Jolokia Chocolate
description: **Bhut Jolokia Chocolate** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili pepper variant boasting **800,000 to 1,001,304 Scoville Heat Units**, wrinkled pods that ripen to a deep chocolate‑brown hue, and a subtle, fruity‑smoky flavour beneath its intense heat. It’s typically used in extreme hot sauces, powders and chilli‑enthusiast cultivations. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bhut-jolokia-chocolate/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bhut Jolokia Chocolate” merges heritage and hue: “Bhut Jolokia” (Assamese: ভূত জলকীয়া) means *“ghost pepper”*, a nod to its haunting heat, while “Chocolate” describes the **natural dark‑brown coloration** of this rare variant. Originating in **northeast India** (Assam, Nagaland, Manipur), it’s considered a natural mutation of the original Red Bhut Jolokia. The name thus honours both its **fiery lineage** and its **distinctive colour variant**, making it instantly recognisable among super‑hot chilli aficionados. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Chocolate_Ghost_Chile_Pepper_14968.php))

name: Komodo Dragon
description: **Komodo Dragon pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili pepper developed in the UK, with a heat range of approximately **1,400,000–2,200,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features small, wrinkled pods that mature from green to red and deliver a fruity flavour followed by a delayed, intense burn. It was introduced commercially in 2015 and marketed as one of the world’s hottest peppers. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/komodo-dragon-chili-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Komodo Dragon”** draws on both its intense heat and symbolic imagery. It was bred by UK grower Salvatore Genovese in Bedfordshire and named after the fierce Indonesian lizard, suggesting the pepper’s dominating power. The dramatic moniker reflects its status as the hottest commercially grown pepper in the UK at its debut, and conjures the idea of fiery, dragon‑like breath. Its name emphasises both **extreme potency** and **mythical intimidation**.

name: 7 Pot Douglah
description: **7 Pot Douglah** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a rare and extremely hot chocolate‑brown chili pepper from Trinidad, ranking between **923,889 and 1,853,986 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Its pods are wrinkled, about 2 inches long, and mature from green to dark chocolate brown. The flavor is fruity with nutty and earthy undertones, but it’s the intense, lingering heat that defines this cultivar—used primarily in super‑hot sauces and chili challenges ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/7-pot-douglah-chili-peppers/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-douglah/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Douglah”** combines the Trinidadian “7 Pot” prefix—meaning one pepper is enough to season **seven cooking pots**—with “Douglah,” a local term meaning **“mixed race person”**, referencing the pepper’s brown coloration. “Douglah” in Trinidadian slang reflects its distinctive chocolate brown hue, setting it apart from the red 7 Pot varieties. The naming thus honours both its legendary heat‑capacity and its cultural roots in Trinidadian chili‑growing tradition ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-douglah/)).

name: Naga Morich
description: **Naga Morich** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **extremely hot chili pepper** from **northeastern India and Bangladesh**, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **800,000 to over 1,000,000 SHU**. It produces small, wrinkled, bright red pods with a **fruity, slightly smoky flavor**. Primarily used in hot sauces, powders, and spicy dishes, it is recognized as one of the hottest peppers in the world ([SuperHotChiles](https://www.superhotchiles.com/product/naga-morich-pepper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/naga-morich/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Naga Morich”** combines the **ethnic Naga people of India’s northeast** and the word *“morich”*, meaning **chili pepper** in Bengali. It highlights the pepper’s **geographic and cultural roots**. Known locally for its **exceptional heat**, it is often used in traditional cooking to spice stews and chutneys. The name preserves both **regional identity and fiery reputation**, reflecting its status as a historic and formidable chili ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/naga-morich/)).

name: Infinity Pepper
description: **Infinity Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a super‑hot chili pepper developed in the UK by breeder Nick Woods of Fire Foods in Grantham, England. It registers around **1,067,286 to 1,250,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)** and features bulbous, wrinkled pods that ripen from green to bright red. Its flavour has a short fruity prelude that is quickly overwhelmed by a severe delayed‑burn effect, making it a favourite among enthusiasts of extreme heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/infinity-pepper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/infinity-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Infinity Pepper”** reflects both its **boundary‑pushing heat** and its experimental origins. The term “Infinity” was chosen to suggest the unimaginable or limitless intensity of its spiciness. It was bred by Nick Woods after a spontaneous greenhouse hybridisation, and for a brief time in 2011 it held the world record for the hottest chili pepper. The name signals a blend of ambition and extremity—a chili that goes beyond conventional limits. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infinity_chili))

name: Ghost Pepper
description: **Ghost Pepper** (*Bhut Jolokia*, *Capsicum chinense*) is an **ultra-hot chili pepper** from **northeastern India (Assam, Nagaland, Manipur)**, with heat ranging from **800,000 to over 1,000,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces small, wrinkled red pods with a **fruity, slightly smoky flavor** beneath its extreme heat. It is used in **hot sauces, powders, and spicy dishes** and is recognized worldwide as a super-hot chili ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_pepper), [ChiliMafia](https://www.chilimafia.com/en/chilli/ghost-pepper-bhut-jolokia)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Ghost Pepper”** comes from the Assamese term *Bhut Jolokia*, meaning **“ghost chili”**, a reference to its **potent heat that sneaks up like a ghost** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_pepper)). Traditionally used in northeast Indian cuisine, it is valued for both **culinary and cultural significance**. The English nickname highlights its **elusive, creeping spiciness**, which helped it gain international fame and recognition among super-hot chili enthusiasts worldwide ([ChiliMafia](https://www.chilimafia.com/en/chilli/ghost-pepper-bhut-jolokia)).

name: 7 Pot Brown
description: **7 Pot Brown Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a super‑hot, chocolate‑brown chili pepper native to Trinidad. Its pods are heavily wrinkled, about 2–3 cm long, ripening from lime‑green to dark brown. With heat levels often exceeding **900,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it offers a fruity, nutty undertone before unleashing a fierce burn, making it ideal for hot sauces, dried chili powders, or extreme‑heat culinary use. ([Chili-Shop24](https://www.chili-shop24.de/chili-zucht/chili-samen/schaerfegrad-10/12008/7-pot-brown-chili-samen))
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Brown”** comes from the famous “7 Pot” prefix used in Trinidad, suggesting **one pepper is enough to spice seven pots of stew**. “Brown” refers to the unique chocolate‑brown colour of this variant, distinguishing it from red or yellow 7 Pot cultivars. The pepper’s heritage traces back to Trinidadian farms where growers selected for both extreme heat and the brown hue. Today, its name blends **legendary spiciness and distinctive appearance**, offering both cultural significance and dramatic flavour. ([Scovilla](https://www.scovilla.com/en/7-Pot-Brown-Chili-Samen/900031))

name: Trinidad Scorpion "Butch-T"
description: **Trinidad Scorpion ‘Butch T’** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili pepper famed for its blistering heat—measuring up to around **1.46 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It bears vivid red, heavily wrinkled pods with a distinctive stinger‑like tip, and delivers a short fruity flavour followed by an immediate, intense burn. Originally released around 2011, it remains a prized variety among extreme‑hot pepper enthusiasts and sauce creators ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/trinidad-scorpion-butch-t-chili-pepper/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-butch-t/)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Butch T” pays tribute to **Butch Taylor**, the U.S. hot‑sauce maker and pepper breeder who helped propagate the variety, while “Trinidad Scorpion” references its Trinidadian super‑hot pepper lineage and the pod’s **sting‑like tail** that resembles a scorpion’s barb. The pepper briefly held the title of **world’s hottest chili** in 2011, adding legend to its name. Its origin story encompasses Trinidad, Mississippi and Australia—making it a global icon of extreme heat ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-butch-t/)).

name: 7 Pot Jonah
description: **7 Pot Jonah Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super‑hot chili cultivar** from Trinidad, typically ranging between **800,000 and 1,200,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features bold, wrinkled pods that transition from green to vibrant red, with a pronounced **fruity and tropical flavor**. Its size is somewhat larger than many 7 Pot variants, making it especially prized for **hot sauces, salsas**, or any dish requiring serious heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-jonah/), [SuperHotChiles](https://www.superhotchiles.com/product/trinidad-7-pot-jonah/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Jonah”** merges a legendary heat descriptor and a personal touch. The “7 Pot” prefix alludes to the myth that one pepper can spice **seven pots of stew**—a nod to extreme potency. “Jonah” is believed to reference an early breeder or distributor who popularized this strain, distinguishing it among 7 Pot family cultivars. The name thus captures both the pepper’s **myth‑level heat** and its **distinct identity** within the fiery world of super‑hots.

name: Dundicut
description: **Dundicut Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, round chili pepper, typically ½‑1 inch in diameter, with dark red pods. It originates from Pakistan’s Sindh region and ripens to a rich ruby hue. Measuring around **55,000–65,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it offers a full‑bodied flavour—fruity, slightly smoky, and robust—while providing moderate heat, perfect for drying, crushing, or spicing dishes. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/dundicut-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Dundicut” (also spelled “Dundicutt” or “Dundike”) comes from its local name in the Sindh province of Pakistan. It’s sometimes called *“gol lal mirch”* in local languages, meaning “round red pepper”. The pepper has been grown traditionally in the arid Thar region for generations, often sun‑dried and stored for winter use. Fun fact: a single crushed Dundicut can flavour two cooking pots, highlighting its concentrated flavour and moderate heat punch. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dundicut))

name: Pequin
description: **Pequin Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum var. glabriusculum*) is a small yet fiery chili pepper from Mexico, typically rating **30,000–60,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Its tiny pods are about 2–4 cm long, ripening from green to vibrant red, and deliver a **citrusy, nutty, and smoky** flavour. Often used in salsas, sauces, and dried for seasoning, they pack a flavourful punch despite their size ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/chili-pequin-chili-peppers/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pequin_pepper)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Pequin” likely stems from the Spanish word *pequeño*, meaning **“small”**, a nod to the pepper’s diminutive size. It is known by many regional names such as *chile petín*, *bird pepper*, and *mashito*, among others, across Mexico and Central America. This pepper grows wild in Mexico’s forests and has been harvested for centuries. A fun fact: it’s often so spicy that **a few peppers can season an entire pot of stew**, exactly like its “7 Pot” cousins emphasise—just in miniature form. ([Sonoran Spice Guide](https://www.sonoranspice.com/blogs/sonoran-spice-product-guides/pequin-peppers), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pequin_pepper)).

name: Tabasco
description: **Tabasco Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a small, slender chili pepper, measuring 2–3 cm in length, native to **Mexico and Central America**. It ripens from green to bright red, delivering a **sharp, tangy, and moderately hot flavor** of around **30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It is the primary ingredient in the famous **Tabasco® hot sauce**, prized worldwide for its vibrant heat and distinct taste ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/tabasco-chili-peppers/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tabasco_pepper)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Tabasco”** comes from the Mexican state of **Tabasco**, where the pepper was first cultivated extensively. It became famous globally when **Edmund McIlhenny** used it in 1868 to produce the original Tabasco® hot sauce. Fun fact: the pepper’s **high capsaicin content** and small size make it perfect for sauces that can last for decades without losing flavor. Its name now signifies both **heritage and iconic heat** ([Tabasco® Official](https://www.tabasco.com/our-story/)).

name: Aurora
description: **Aurora Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, upright ornamental chili pepper that dazzles with a **rainbow of colors**—pods ripen from green to lavender, deep purple, orange, and finally red. With a heat level of approximately **30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it delivers a respectable spicy kick comparable to a cayenne, though its true appeal lies in its vibrant aesthetic. Compact and ideal for containers, it suits patios or windowsills beautifully. ([Thresh Seed Co](https://www.threshseed.com/products/aurora), [Vertiloom](https://www.vertiloom.com/en/aurora-seeds.html))
aditionalInfo: The name “Aurora” is derived from the Latin word for “dawn,” chosen to reflect the pepper’s **brilliant, shifting hues** reminiscent of the dawn sky. These ornamental plants display multiple fruit colors simultaneously, giving the effect of miniature lanterns on the plant. Though developed for decorative appeal, Aurora peppers are **edible and moderately hot**, offering a fun dual-use of flair and flavor. They also make charming festive accents or spice additions.

name: Aji Amarillo
description: **Ají Amarillo** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a medium‑hot Peruvian chili rated around 30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU), featuring long, tapered pods that ripen from green to a vivid orange‑yellow. It delivers a fruity, tropical flavor with hints of passion fruit and mango, making it ideal for **sauces, stews, and ceviche** in Peruvian cuisine. ([The Spruce Eats](https://www.thespruceeats.com/aji-amarillo-peruvian-yellow-chile-pepper-3029288))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Ají Amarillo”** literally translates to “yellow chili” in Spanish — “ají” meaning chili, and “amarillo” meaning yellow. This name reflects both the pepper’s traditional use and its vibrant color when cooked. Native to Peru and cultivated since ancient times, Ají Amarillo is a culinary cornerstone in Peruvian dishes such as *ají de gallina* and *papa a la huancaína*. Its name and flavor both carry deep **cultural significance** and gastronomic appeal. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/aji-amarillo-chili-peppers/))

name: Black Cobra
description: **Black Cobra Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an eye‑catching chili variety with pods that grow upright and ripen through green → jet‑black → red. Its plants feature silver‑tinged hairs on leaves and stems, giving it a “furry” look. The heat level is moderate, around 20,000–40,000 Scoville units, making it significantly hotter than a jalapeño ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/black-cobra-pepper/)). Ideal for container growing or ornamental display, it also works dried in spice blends.
aditionalInfo: The name “Black Cobra” arises from the pepper’s astonishing vertical stance and dark colour: the pods stand erect like a cobra ready to strike, especially in their black stage. It’s sometimes called “Goat’s Weed” in certain regions due to its wild appearance ([White on Rice Couple](https://whiteonricecouple.com/black-cobra-goats-weed-pepper/)). Its unique visual appeal—changing colour and upright posture—makes it a favourite for gardeners seeking dramatic chili plants. While not the fiercest in terms of heat, its playful name and striking look ensure it stands out in any chilli collection.

name: Jwala Finger Hot
description: **Jwala Finger Hot Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an elongated chili from India, measuring about 2–3 inches long and boasting a heat range of approximately **20,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Its thin, glossy pods ripen from light green to bright red, delivering a sharp but manageable heat and a slightly sweet, pungent flavor. Often used fresh or dried in curries, chutneys and pickles across Indian households. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/jwala-finger-hot-chili-peppers/), [TomatoFifou](https://www.tomatofifou.com/en/produit/jwala-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Jwala” comes from the Hindi word for **“flame”**, highlighting the pepper’s fiery character, while “Finger Hot” denotes its slender, finger‑like shape and hot nature. Originally cultivated in regions like Gujarat in India, Jwala peppers have become a staple in Indian cuisine. They’re sometimes called “Finger Hot” in export markets to reflect their shape and medium‑level spiciness. Fun fact: despite its modest size, the Jwala’s flavor is intense—just a few pods can brighten a stew or spice blend significantly!

name: Japones
description: **Japones Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, medium‑hot chili measuring about 2 inches long and ½ inch wide. It ripens from green to red and typically clocks in at **15,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it about 3–6 times hotter than a typical jalapeño. Its flavor is simply spicy and clean—ideal for adding heat without complex taste notes in stir‑fries, infusions, or flakes. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/japones-pepper/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/japones-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Japones”** means “Japanese” in Spanish—reflecting the pepper’s use in Asian and Latin cuisine, rather than its actual origin. While the species originates in the Americas, this cultivar became popular in Asian and Caribbean markets under the name “Japones.” Some also call it *Hontaka* or *Santaka*. Fun fact: its thin shape and solid heat make it perfect for infusing oils and spirits, and it’s commonly used as a milder alternative to chile de árbol. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/japones-pepper/))

name: Lemon Drop
description: **Lemon Drop Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a vibrant, citrus‑tinged chili pepper native to Peru, rated around **30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are slender (about 4–10 cm long), ripen from green to bright yellow, and deliver a **fruity, lemon‑like flavor** with moderate heat—ideal for salsas, sauces, or drying into a striking yellow chile powder. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lemon_drop_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Lemon Drop” (also known as *Ají Limón*) highlights both its **bright yellow color** and **zesty citrus aroma**. “Ají” means chili in many South American languages, while “Limón” means lemon in Spanish—so the name literally means “lemon chili.” Historically, the pepper comes from the Andean region of Peru and only became widely cultivated outside South America in the 1990s. Fun fact: due to its thin‑walled fruits, it dries quickly and is often ground into a vivid yellow chile powder that adds both heat and color to dishes. ([Chili‑Werkstatt](https://chiliwerkstatt.com/chilisorten/lemon-drop/))

name: Black Pearl
description: **Black Pearl Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum* ‘Black Pearl’) is a striking ornamental chili with glossy black‑purple foliage and small, round fruits that mature from green to jet‑black and finally bright red. While primarily grown for decorative appeal, the pods are edible and register around **10,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. This compact plant thrives in containers or garden beds, offering both vivid colour and moderate spice. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/black-pearl-pepper/), [RHS](https://www.rhs.org.uk/plants/278248/capsicum-annuum-black-pearl/details))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Black Pearl”** reflects the pepper’s jewel‐like appearance: the dark pods resemble lustrous pearls against the deep foliage. Developed in the United States by researchers Rob Griesbach and John Stommel at the U.S. National Arboretum, the cultivar won the All‑America Selections (AAS) award in 2006 for its exceptional ornamental value. Fun fact: the leaves frequently appear nearly black in direct sunlight, making it a favourite for dramatic contrasts in garden design. ([Chili‑Plants](https://chili-plants.com/en/chilli-varieties/chili-black-pearl/?srsltid=AfmBOoo4KWyfh7Zbiczw3K3ihcGvoRxYjFXz2g9gvOd-9p1xFFBqdpg))

name: Manzano
description: **Manzano Pepper** (*Capsicum pubescens*) is a distinctive chili from Central and South America, recognized for its *apple‑shaped* pods that mature from green to yellow‑orange or red. Rated around **10,000–30,000 Scoville units**, it delivers a fruity‑citrus flavor with moderate heat, and features uncommon traits like **black seeds** and hairy leaves. Ideal fresh in salsas or stuffed dishes, this pepper thrives in cooler, high‑altitude climates. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/manzano-peppers/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/manzano-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Manzano”** comes from the Spanish word for **“apple‑tree”** (*manzana*), referencing the pepper’s rounded form and apple‑like appearance. Indigenous to the Andes and cultivated for centuries, it belongs to the unusual species *Capsicum pubescens*, which tolerates cooler temperatures. Fun fact: unlike many peppers, manzanos have **black seeds** and hairy foliage, and were used by pre‑Incan peoples. Their unique traits and naming reflect a rich cultural and botanical heritage. ([Nature’s Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Manzana_Chile_Peppers_256.php), [Cayenne Diane](https://www.cayennediane.com/peppers/manzano-pepper/))

name: Chile de Arbol
description: **Chile de Árbol Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, bright red chili from Mexico, typically **15–25 cm long**, with a heat range of **15,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It has a **smoky, nutty flavor** and is commonly used dried in sauces, salsas, and as a spice in cooking. Its long, thin pods make it perfect for infusing oils or creating chili flakes ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/chile-de-arbol-chili-peppers/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chile_de_árbol)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Chile de Árbol”** translates from Spanish as **“tree chili”**, referring to the upright growth habit of its branches, resembling tiny trees laden with red fruits. Native to Mexico, it has been a staple in Mexican cuisine for centuries. Fun fact: its pods retain heat and flavor when dried, making it a popular choice for long‑lasting chili powders and sauces. The name captures both its **botanical growth form** and **culinary prominence** ([spicesinc](https://spicesinc.com/blogs/what-are-chiles-de-arbol?srsltid=AfmBOoqGIZI_xeHZwGj4YmoAYU3QCAYBDEpaUIe5s6yDSZN8Gm7fse0P)).

name: Bishop's Crown
description: **Bishop’s Crown Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a uniquely shaped chili pepper with wing‑like lobes and an average heat range of **5,000–30,000 SHU**, offering a mild to moderate kick. Its pods are roughly 1 inch long and 2–3 inches wide, mature from green to red, and deliver a **sweet, fruity flavor**. Ideal for stuffing, pickling, or adding visual flair in salads, it’s both culinary and decorative. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/bishops-crown-chili-peppers/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bishops-crown-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bishop’s Crown” refers to the pepper’s **distinctive three‑sided lobed shape**, reminiscent of a bishop’s ceremonial hat (called a mitre). It’s also known by fun nicknames such as “Christmas Bell,” “Joker’s Hat,” or “Friar’s Hat,” thanks to its whimsical form. Originally from South America and popularised in Barbados, its visual appeal and manageable heat have made it a favourite among growers and chefs alike—especially for elegant stuffed‑pepper applications. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bishop%27s_crown), [My Chilli Garden](https://mychilligarden.com/bishops-crown/))

name: Hinkelhatz
description: **Hinkelhatz Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, conical hot pepper cultivated for over 150 years by Pennsylvania Dutch growers in the United States. With a heat range of roughly **5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it is significantly hotter than a jalapeño but remains manageable for many. The fruits, about 1–2 inches long, ripen from green to orange‑red and are frequently used for pickling, hot pepper vinegar, and garden ornamentals. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/hinkelhatz-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [Heritage Harvest Seed](https://heritageharvestseed.com/products/hinkelhatz-pepper-seeds))
aditionalInfo: The name “Hinkelhatz” comes from the Pennsylvania German (Pennsylvania Dutch) term meaning **“chicken heart.”** This name references the pepper’s roughly heart‑shaped appearance—small, stout, and pointed at the tip. It’s deeply tied to the Pennsylvania Dutch tradition of preserving garden produce and crafting regional condiments like pepper vinegars. Fun fact: despite its modest size, a single Hinkelhatz can deliver a surprising bite, making it a favourite among enthusiasts seeking flavour and heat in heritage varieties. ([A Gardener’s Table](https://agardenerstable.com/a-hot-pepper-for-cool-climes/))

name: Black Prince
description: **Black Prince Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) delivers ornamental elegance with edible value. This compact plant features deep purple‑black foliage and pods starting black, then transitioning to red. Fruits are small (around 1/2″) and produce a moderate heat—roughly 5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units—comparable to a cayenne pepper. The flavor is slightly sweet, making it suitable for salads or mild sautéed dishes while also serving beautifully in containers or garden borders. ([Pepper Joe’s](https://pepperjoe.com/products/black-prince-pepper-seeds?srsltid=AfmBOordfGWyDV6CXMEltNijfLGtN92T5AEbcf5MtUZRtKVir), [The Hippy Seed Company](https://thehippyseedcompany.com/product/black-prince/))
aditionalInfo: Its regal name “Black Prince” arises from the pepper’s rich, dark colour and noble appearance, suggesting a royal standing among edible plants. The black foliage and pods resemble a royal robe, while the later red‑ripened fruit nod to a crown’s jewels. Often promoted as an ornamental variety, it also honours its edible status—balancing aesthetic flair and garden utility. A fun fact: its striking dark leaves develop especially under strong light, creating a dramatic “miniature dark tree laden with jewels” effect in containers or raised beds. ([Chile Pepper Institute](https://chilepepperinstitute.ecwid.com/Black-Prince-p616732192))

name: Count Dracula
description: **[Count Dracula Pepper]()** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a striking ornamental chili sought for its dramatic appearance. It typically ranges from **5,000 to 30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, delivering a mild to moderate burn. The plant features black‑purple foliage, deep purple flowers, and pods that ripen from jet‑black to vivid red—ideal for containers and Halloween‑themed gardens. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/count-dracula-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Count Dracula”** was chosen for its gothic aesthetic and vampiric appeal: the chili’s **dark leaves and pods** bring to mind the legendary Transylvanian vampire. The “Count” reference matches the regal title and the pepper’s dramatic presentation, while the “Dracula” element evokes mystery and night‑time intrigue. Fun fact: the pods are edible, although grown primarily for ornamentation, and the cultivar makes a bold visual statement alongside its modest heat. ([The Hippy Seed Company](https://thehippyseedcompany.com/product/count-dracula-chilli/))

name: Bulgarian Carrot
description: This striking heirloom pepper from Bulgaria features bright orange, carrot‑shaped pods about 3 to 4 inches long, ripening from green to vivid orange. With a heat range of approximately 5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units, it sits in the medium‑hot category and delivers a sweet‐fruity flavor with a crisp texture, ideal for roasting, pickling or salsas. Its crunchy skin and vibrant color make it as decorative as it is edible. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bulgarian-carrot-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bulgarian Carrot” owes both to its country of origin—Bulgaria—and its resemblance to a carrot in shape and bright orange color. Known locally as *Shipka* or *Shifka*, the pepper was cultivated near Bulgaria’s Black Sea region and reportedly smuggled out of the Iron Curtain era in the 1980s. Its crunchy flesh makes it exceptional for roasting and pickling, while its Eastern European heritage and striking appearance give it a compelling back‑story. ([NatureWord](https://www.natureword.com/what-are-bulgarian-carrot-peppers/))

name: Fish Pepper
description: **Fish Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a versatile heirloom chili producing small pods that ripen through hues of white with green stripes, orange, brown, and red, and deliver medium heat — roughly **5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The plants also display striking green‑and‑white variegated foliage and are a favourite for both culinary use and ornamental value. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fish_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Fish Pepper” is rooted in the pepper’s historical use in **Baltimore and Philadelphia seafood houses** during the late 19th and early 20th centuries—particularly in crab, oyster, and fish dishes in African‑American communities. ([Epicurious article](https://www.epicurious.com/ingredients/fish-peppers-african-american-garden-article)) A fun fact: some immature pods remain pale white, making them ideal for spicing white sauces without altering the colour. The cultivar nearly vanished mid‑20th century but was revived thanks to seed‑saving efforts, making it both a culinary gem and a piece of cultural heritage.

name: Sport
description: **Sport Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, 1–1.5 inch long chili pepper, typically pickled in vinegar, and boasting a heat range of approximately **10,000–23,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. They are commonly used in jars for toppings and condiments, especially in hot dogs, sandwiches, and giardiniera. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sport-peppers/) , [The Spruce Eats](https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-sport-peppers-4777768))
aditionalInfo: The name “Sport Pepper” is strongly associated with its prominent use on the iconic **Chicago‑style hot dog**, where a jar of these bright green pickled peppers is a standard topping—hence “sport” in the sense of matching the fast‑moving game‑day vibe. ([The Takeout](https://www.thetakeout.com/what-is-a-sport-pepper-on-chicago-style-hot-dogs-1849332297/)) The pepper’s exact cultivar origin is unclear, but its usage in the Chicago region has made it a culinary icon. Fun fact: though commonly pickled and jarred, fresh sport peppers are rare in supermarkets outside the Midwest.

name: Peter Pepper
description: **Peter Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a quirky heirloom chili pepper known for its distinctly phallic‑shaped pods, which typically measure 3–4 inches long and 1–1.5 inches wide. With a heat range of approximately **10,000–25,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it’s hotter than a jalapeño but still manageable. Grown primarily in East Texas and Louisiana, it’s used for pickling, salsa, or just as a conversation‑starter in the garden. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_pepper), [Victoriananna](https://victoriananursery.co.uk/Chilli-Pepper-Seed-Peter-Pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The **name "Peter Pepper"** is a **euphemistic reference** to the pepper's **unmistakably phallic shape** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/peter-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Also known as **"Penis Pepper"** or **"Chilly Willy,"** this **heirloom variety** has uncertain origins but has been cultivated in **Texas** and **Louisiana** for generations. The Peter Pepper is famous for its **anatomically suggestive appearance** and is a **popular novelty gift** and **garden curiosity**. Despite its humorous shape, it produces **flavorful peppers** suitable for culinary use.

name: Hungarian Wax
description: **Hungarian Wax Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a vibrant heirloom chili measuring about 10–15 cm long and 2–3 cm wide. It typically ripens from a pale yellow to orange‑red, and boasts a heat range of approximately **1,000–15,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**—mild compared to super‑hots. With its waxy, glossy skin and thick flesh, it’s ideal for pickling, stuffing, slicing raw, or using in stews and sauces. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hungarian_wax_pepper), [The Hot Sauce Guy](https://thehotsauceguy.ca/hungarian-wax-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Hungarian Wax” reflects its heritage in Hungary and its **waxy sheen** when picked at the right stage—early yellow fruit displaying a translucent, smooth surface. Though peppers are native to the Americas, this cultivar became a staple in Hungarian agriculture and cuisine when introduced in the 16th century. Fun fact: when fully ripe to orange‑red the heat increases significantly, and the pepper was instrumental in the production of traditional Hungarian paprika. Its name carries both **geographic provenance** and distinct visual description. ([Nature’s Produce](https://naturesproduce.com/encyclopedia/hungarian-wax/))

name: Aleppo
description: **Aleppo Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a moderately hot chili variety, rated around **10,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods ripen to a deep burgundy color, are partially dried, deseeded and crushed into flakes or coarse powder. They offer a fruity, raisin‑like aroma with mild heat—ideal for Mediterranean and Middle Eastern dishes. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleppo_pepper), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/aleppo-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Aleppo Pepper” derives from the ancient city of Aleppo (Halab in Arabic) in northern Syria, which was once a major node on the Silk Road and a historical center for spice production and trade. These peppers were traditionally grown in that region, sun‑dried, then crushed with salt and oil to create the distinctive spice used locally. A fun fact: due to the ongoing conflict and crop disruptions in Syria, much of the “Aleppo pepper” sold internationally is now grown and processed in Turkey, yet still retains the name and flavor profile. ([Scientific American](https://www.scientificamerican.com/blog/food-matters/what-s-an-aleppo-pepper/))

name: Fresno
description: **Fresno Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a medium‑heat chili developed in the 1950s by grower Clarence “Brownie” Hamlin in Fresno County, California ([Eater](https://www.eater.com/2018/7/11/17537702/fresno-chili-pepper-history-taste) & [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-heat-chili-peppers/fresno-chili-peppers/)). Measuring roughly 2‑3 inches long, it ripens from green to bright red and delivers 2,500‑10,000 SHU. With thinner walls than a jalapeño, it’s ideal for sauces, salsas, pickling or daily cooking.
aditionalInfo: The name “Fresno” honours its origin: the fertile agricultural region of Fresno County in California’s Central Valley. Hamlin named the variety after his home turf when releasing it commercially in 1952. Fun fact: despite superficial similarity to a jalapeño, Fresnos often have a fruitier, slightly smoky flavour and thinner walls, making them popular in contemporary cuisine. It’s also dubbed a “gateway chile” because of its balance of bold flavour and approachable heat. ([Nature’s Produce](https://naturesproduce.com/encyclopedia/fresno-chile/))

name: Hatch
description: **Hatch Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a flavorful green chile known for its thick flesh and mild to moderate heat, typically around 3,000–8,000 Scoville units. Commonly harvested while still green, it’s excellent for roasting, stuffing, and using in salsas when fresh, or canning and preserving after roasting. Its flavor is often described as sweet, smoky, and slightly earthy—ideal for Southwestern and Tex‑Mex cuisine. ([Chile Pepper Institute](https://cpi.nmsu.edu/chile-info/then-and-now.html), [New Mexico Magazine](https://www.newmexicomagazine.org/blog/post/hatch-green-chile/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Hatch Pepper” comes from the fertile agricultural region of Hatch, New Mexico and its valley, where the unique soil, altitude, and climate produce chiles with a distinct “roasted green” flavor. Technically, “Hatch” is not a specific cultivar but a geographic designation for a variety of chiles grown in that region. Fun fact: the town celebrates its harvest with the annual Hatch Chile Festival each Labor Day weekend, and only chiles actually grown in the Hatch Valley can legally carry the “Hatch” name in New Mexico. ([New Mexico Magazine](https://www.newmexicomagazine.org/blog/post/hatch-green-chile/), [Village of Hatch](https://villageofhatch.org/arts_and_culture/history_of_hatch))

name: Puya
description: **Puya Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a moderately hot Mexican chili valued for both its flavor and versatility. The dried pods measure around 3–4 inches long, ripen to a crimson or dark red, and deliver a heat range of about **5,000‑8,000 Scoville Heat Units**. They carry a fruity, cherry‑and‑licorice flavor profile, making them ideal for smoky sauces, chili oils, soups, and stews. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/puya-chili-peppers/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/puya-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Puya” is derived from the Spanish/Mexican term **“puya” or “pulla”**, which translates roughly to **“goad” or “prod”**, alluding to the chili’s sharp kick. It originates in Mexico’s Central Valley region, including parts of Michoacán and Jalisco, where it has been used for centuries in traditional cuisine ([Spices Inc. blog](https://spicesinc.com/blogs/what-are-puya-chiles)). A fun fact: although it resembles the popular Guajillo Pepper, the Puya is smaller and significantly hotter—often twice the heat of a typical guajillo. It’s also sometimes mistaken for a dried Chile de Árbol but offers a more complex, fruity flavor layered beneath its heat. ([GrowHotPeppers guide](https://growhotpeppers.com/chile-puya/))

name: Chipotle
description: **Chipotle Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a smoked and dried variant of the red jalapeño chili, offering a medium heat of roughly **2,500–8,000 Scoville Heat Units**. Its smoky, earthy, slightly sweet flavor is a staple in Mexican and Southwestern cuisine, perfect for salsas, marinades, sauces, and stews. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-heat-chili-peppers/chipotle-chili-peppers/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chipotle))
aditionalInfo: The name “Chipotle” comes from the Nahuatl word **“chilpoctli,”** meaning **“smoked chili”**, reflecting the traditional method of smoking ripe jalapeños over wood fires. Originally cultivated in Mexico, chipotles have been used for centuries to preserve chiles while imparting deep smoky flavors. Fun fact: the drying and smoking process concentrates sugars, giving chipotles a subtle sweetness alongside their moderate heat. They are commonly found canned in adobo sauce, widely used in both classic and modern Mexican recipes. ([National Geographic](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/chipotle))

name: Espelette
description: **Espelette Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*, variety Gorria) is a mild‑heat chili pepper from the Basque region of France. It has a Scoville rating around 1,000‑4,000 SHU, and offers a flavour described as **sweet, fruity, and subtly smoky**, making it ideal for finishing dishes or seasoning traditional Basque recipes. It’s typically harvested late summer, sun‑dried and ground into a vibrant red powder that retains its fragrance and colour. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Espelette_pepper), [SpiceLibrary](https://spicelibrary.org/spice/pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Espelette” comes from the village of Espelette in the Pyrénées‑Atlantiques, where the pepper has been grown since the 16th century. In 2000 it was awarded the AOC (Appellation d’Origine Contrôlée) and in 2002 the PDO status, meaning only peppers grown and processed in that region under specific regulations may bear the name **Piment d’Espelette**. Fun fact: each autumn the houses in the region hang strings of red peppers on their walls to dry, and the local festival in late October celebrates this regional icon of Basque cuisine. ([CooksInfo](https://www.cooksinfo.com/espelette-chile-peppers), [Atelier du Piment d’Espelette](https://www.atelier-du-piment-espelette.fr/en/espelette-chilli-pepper/))

name: Cherry Bomb
description: **Cherry Bomb Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a lively, nearly round chili pepper with a size similar to a cherry. It ripens from green to bright red and has thick flesh with a heat range of about 2,500–5,000 Scoville units—milder than many hot peppers, but noticeable. Its juicy, slightly sweet flavor makes it excellent for pickling, stuffing, or adding to salads and sandwiches. ([Pepper Pulse](https://www.pepperpulse.com/cherry-bomb/), [Cayenne Diane](https://www.cayennediane.com/peppers/cherry-bomb-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cherry Bomb” reflects both its cherry‑like appearance—small, round, bright red—and the “bomb” of flavor it can deliver despite its modest size. Originally popular in Hungary and Italy where it was known as *Peperoncino Ciliegia*, the modern name caught on in English‑speaking markets. Fun fact: its thick walls help it hold up beautifully when pickled or stuffed, making it a favourite in deli‑style jars and gourmet apps. ([Chili‑Planet](https://chili-planet.com/en/products/cherry-bomb), [Vending Business Machine Pro Service](https://vendingproservice.com/cherry-peppers-heat-level-guide-scoville-scale/))

name: Guajillo
description: **Guajillo Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a dried chili with a sleek, dark reddish‑brown skin and a mild‑to‑medium heat range of **2,500–5,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods typically measure 4–6 inches long and have a fruity, tangy, and slightly smoky flavour, making them a staple in Mexican sauces, salsas, moles, and marinades. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/guajillo-pepper/), [Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Dried_Guajillo_Chile_Peppers_794.php))
aditionalInfo: The name “Guajillo” derives from the Spanish word *guajillo*, which is linked to *mirasol* chilies (meaning “looking at the sun”) that stand upright on the plant in Mexico’s north‑central states. When these are sun‑dried, they become the guajillo form; the term celebrates their geographic roots and distinctive drying process. Fun fact: guajillos are considered part of the “holy trinity” of Mexican dried chiles (with ancho and pasilla) and are celebrated for adding rich colour and fruit‑berry accents to traditional dishes like mole and pozole. ([Don Zabor – The Guajillo Chile and Its Origin](https://donzabor.mx/en/blogs/noticias/el-chile-guajillo-y-su-origen?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

name: Numex Big Jim
description: **Numex Big Jim Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a remarkable New Mexican variety producing **giant pods up to 12–14 inches long** with mild heat—typically around **500–3,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Developed in the 1970s at the New Mexico State University’s Chile Pepper Institute, these thick‑walled fruits are ideal for roasting, stuffing, and green‑chile sauces. Its size, flavour and heritage make it a staple among Hatch‑style chiles. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_Jim_pepper), [Cayenne Diane](https://www.cayennediane.com/peppers/numex-big-jim/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Big Jim” honours Jim Lytle, a collaborator with Dr Roy Nakayama in its development. The “NuMex” prefix stands for “New Mexico.” In 1975 the variety was released, and it even listed in the Guinness World Records for producing the world’s longest chilli pods. Fun fact: though the heat is modest for chili enthusiasts, its massive size makes it perfect for classic dishes like chile rellenos, and for roasting in Hatch‑Chile festivals. ([New Mexico Magazine](https://www.newmexicomagazine.org/blog/post/the-mystery-of-numex-big-jim-chile-new-mexico/))

name: Cascabel
description: **Cascabel Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, round chili pepper about 1–2 inches in diameter that ripens from green to dark red‑brown. With a mild to medium heat range of approximately **1,500–3,000 Scoville Heat Units**, it delivers a nutty, earthy flavor with mild smokiness. Commonly used dried in Mexican sauces, stews, and salsas, its smooth skin and versatile heat make it ideal for layering rich flavours without overwhelming heat. ([CooksInfo](https://www.cooksinfo.com/cascabel-peppers), [SpiceLibrary](https://spicelibrary.org/spice/chiles-cascabel))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cascabel” comes from the Spanish word for **“little bell”** or **“rattle”**, reflecting the characteristic **rattling sound** the seeds make inside the dried pod. Also known as *chile bola* (“ball chili”), this cultivar is native to Mexico and has been grown in states such as Coahuila, Durango and Jalisco for generations. Fun fact: it’s one of the few dried chiles whose shape remains spherical after drying, allowing the seeds to rattle freely—hence its musical nickname and unique sensory appeal. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cascabel_chili))

name: Alma Paprika
description: **Alma Paprika Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a Hungarian heirloom cultivated primarily for drying into paprika spice. The pods are squat, 7–10 cm long and 5–7 cm wide, with thick flesh that ripens from white to orange to red. They have a mild to moderate heat level (around 1,000–3,000 SHU) and a sweet, crisp flavor, making them perfect fresh, pickled, or dried into vibrant paprika powder. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Alma_Paprika_Chile_Peppers_13711.php), [PepperJoe](https://pepperjoe.com/products/alma-paprika))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Alma”** means **“apple”** in Hungarian, reflecting the pepper’s rounded, apple‑like shape and substantial body. Originating in Hungary, the variety became essential in the region’s paprika production—especially in the Danube valley homes where peppers were sun‑dried for spice. Fun fact: although mild for chili fans, Alma Paprika’s thick walls and abundant flesh make it ideal for stuffing or grilling, and its powder is a kitchen staple in Hungarian dishes like goulash. ([Tomatofifou](https://www.tomatofifou.com/en/produit/alma-paprika/), [Kokopelli](https://kokopelli-semences.com/en/p/alma-paprika-fort-mid-season-pepper))

name: Italian Long Hot
description: **Italian Long Hot Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an elongated, tapered chili pepper typically 6–8 inches (15–20 cm) long with thin walls and lower heat—often around **100–1,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, though heat can vary. It starts green and ripens to vivid red, offering a sweet, tangy flavor with mild spiciness, making it ideal for frying, roasting, or topping onto sandwiches and pizzas. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/italian-long-hot-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))
aditionalInfo: The name “Italian Long Hot” highlights both its origin and shape: “Italian” refers to its roots in Italy and “Long Hot” describes its elongated form and mild heat. This pepper gained popularity especially in Italian‑American cuisine in regions like Philadelphia and New York. A fun fact: despite the “hot” in its name, it often has less heat than a jalapeño, making it a “gateway” spice. It’s also commonly roasted in olive oil and used as a flavorful topping rather than a fiery filler. ([Spicy Exchange](https://spicyexchange.com/peppers/italian-long-hots/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

name: Cubanelle
description: **Cubanelle Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild sweet pepper typically measuring 4–6 inches long with thin walls and a tapered shape. Its heat level ranges from about **100 to 1,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it much milder than a jalapeño. The flesh is sweet and slightly fruity, and is prized for stuffing, sautéing, or using raw in salads and sandwiches. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/cubanelle-chili-peppers/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/cubanelle-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name *“Cubanelle”* derives from its roots in Caribbean and Latin American cuisine—especially Cuba, where the variety is known as *ají cubanela*. It later gained popularity in Italian cooking under the nickname “Italian frying pepper.” Fun fact: because of its thin walls, Cubanelle peppers fry quickly and develop a rich, sweet flavor; they are a traditional component in *sofrito* bases and feature in stuffed‑pepper dishes across the Dominican Republic, Puerto Rico, and Italian kitchens alike. ([The Spruce Eats](https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-cubanelle-peppers-4776455), [Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Cubanelle_Italian_Green_Chile_Peppers_9955.php))

name: Peppadew
description: **Peppadew Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum ‘Juanita’*) is a sweet‑and‑tangy chili pepper originating from South Africa’s Limpopo province, with a mild heat of around **1,000–1,200 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. These small, bright red to yellow pods are typically pickled and jarred, offering a crisp texture, fruity warmth, and vibrant flavour ideal for antipasti, salads, and stuffed‑pepper snacks ([AllRecipes](https://www.allrecipes.com/article/what-are-peppadew-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Peppadew” is a trademarked brand created by South African company Peppadew International to market the sweet piquanté pepper. It derives from a playful fusion of the words “pepper” and “dew,” evoking the pepper’s sweet freshness and early‑morning harvest glow ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/picuante-peppadew-chili-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Fun fact: discovered in the early 1990s, it quickly gained popularity worldwide—often stuffed with soft cheeses or added to pizzas for its distinctive flavour and color ([The Spruce Eats](https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-peppadews-5212958?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Cajun Belle
description: **Cajun Belle Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a compact pepper producing blocky fruits about 2–3 inches long, which ripen from light green to orange and finally deep red. With a heat range around **500–4,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)** and a median of about 2,250 SHU, it blends a sweet bell‑pepper base with a noticeable but manageable spice. The flavor is rich, crisp, and ideal for salads, stuffing, or fresh salsas. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/cajun-belle-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cajun Belle” evokes the pepper’s **Louisiana‑style heritage**—“Cajun” points to the region’s spicy, Creole‑influenced cuisine, while “Belle” suggests its beauty and table‑appeal. Developed in the U.S., the variety won the All‑America Selections award (AAS) for its hybrid sweet‑and‑spicy profile and early maturity around 60 days. Fun fact: although marketed as mild, some fully ripe fruits have surprised growers with heat closer to a jalapeño. It thrives in containers and small gardens, making it a go‑to for both heat lovers and ornamental growers. ([Halifax Seed](https://www.halifaxseed.ca/cajun-belle))

name: Pepperoncini
description: **Pepperoncini Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild, tangy chili pepper, typically 2–3 inches long, ripening from green to yellow. With a heat of only **100–500 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it’s very mild, featuring crisp flesh and a slightly sweet, tangy flavor. Pepperoncini are often pickled and served in salads, sandwiches, or antipasti, or used as a garnish for pizzas and Mediterranean dishes
aditionalInfo: The name “Pepperoncini” comes from Italian, meaning **“small peppers”** (*peperoni* is plural for “peppers”), and refers to their slender, elongated shape. Originating in Southern Italy, pepperoncini became popular internationally through Italian cuisine. Fun fact: despite their mild heat, they provide a refreshing crunch and slight tang from pickling, making them a staple in Greek, Italian, and American Mediterranean-style dishes. These peppers are often sold jarred in brine, preserving their color and flavor ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/pepperoncini-chili-peppers/)).

name: Pimiento
description: **Pimiento Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a sweet, heart‑shaped chili typically 3–4 inches long and bright red when ripe. With very mild heat—around **100–500 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**—it’s prized for stuffing (as in green olives), roasting, and making pimiento paste for cheeses and spreads. Its thick, juicy flesh offers a sweet aromatic flavour ideal for salads, sandwiches and sauces. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Pimento_Chile_Peppers_10953.php))
aditionalInfo: The name “Pimiento” comes from the Spanish word *pimiento*, meaning “pepper,” reflecting its European introduction and long‑cultivated status in Spain. Fun fact: the pepper is famous for its use in canned green olives, providing them with a sweet, tangy core. It is also a key ingredient in pimiento cheese, a Southern U.S. classic. Its mild sweetness and striking colour make it popular for pickling and roasting as well as decorative culinary uses. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/pimiento-pepper/))

name: Carmen Italian Sweet
description: **Carmen Italian Sweet Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an elongated sweet‐pepper cultivar, typically 5–6 inches long, that ripens from green to deep red. With **virtually no heat (0 SHU)**, it offers a sweet, fruity flavor and thick flesh ideal for roasting, stuffing, or grilling. Its high yield and early maturity make it a garden favorite. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/carmen-pepper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/carmen-italian-sweet-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Carmen” was inspired by the heroine of the French opera *Carmen* and reflects its Italian frying pepper roots (Corno di Toro type). Developed by Johnny's Selected Seeds, it won the All‑America Selections award in 2006 for its performance and flavor. Fun fact: despite its slender, chilli‑like shape, the Carmen pepper is completely sweet, making it a standout among bell‐type peppers and perfect for creative cooking applications. ([All‑America Selections](https://www.all-americaselections.org/product/pepper-carmen/))

name: Banana Pepper
description: **Banana Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild, elongated chili pepper, typically 6–9 inches long, ripening from pale yellow to orange or red. Its heat ranges from **0–500 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it very mild and suitable for pickling, sandwiches, salads, and pizza toppings. The flavor is tangy and slightly sweet, and the thin-walled pods are easy to slice or stuff, [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/banana-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Banana Pepper” comes from its curved, elongated shape and bright yellow color resembling a banana. Also called *yellow wax pepper*, it originated in the United States and became popular in mid-20th century gardens. Fun fact: Banana peppers are often pickled and sometimes confused with Hungarian wax peppers; their mild heat makes them ideal for kids and spice-sensitive eaters. They are also used in relishes, salsas, and stuffed pepper recipes ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/banana-peppers/)).

name: Santa Fe Grande
description: **Santa Fe Grande Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild‑heat chili cultivar producing upright conical pods about 2–5″ long, which ripen from pale yellow through orange to bright red. With a Scoville rating of roughly **500–700 SHU**, it offers a sweet, fruity flavor and is excellent for pickling, fresh use, or light sautéing. The plants are prolific and compact—ideal for gardens or patio containers. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/santa-fe-grande-chili-peppers/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Fe_Grande))
aditionalInfo: The name “Santa Fe Grande” reflects both its **heritage and place of origin**—part of the New Mexico chile lineage and associated with the Santa Fe region in the U.S. Southwest. The term “Grande” emphasizes the pepper’s upright growth habit and generous yields. A fun fact: this variety is also known as “yellow hot chili” or “guero chili,” and though mild, its vibrant color shift makes it popular as an ornamental as well as culinary pepper. It dates from the 1960s and is valued for its early maturity and versatility. ([Rare Seeds](https://www.rareseeds.com/pepper-hot-santa-fe-grande?srsltid=AfmBOorZX7l0E57K8KH1I9L6avEELNqiK9))

name: Italian Sweet
description: **Italian Sweet Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*, often the heirloom **Corno di Toro “Bull’s Horn” type*) is a versatile, mild‑flavour pepper averaging about **0–500 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features long, tapered pods that begin green and ripen to yellow, orange, or red. With thin walls and a bright sweet taste, it’s ideal for sautéing, roasting, stuffing, or eating fresh in salads. ([Gardening Know How](https://www.gardeningknowhow.com/edible/vegetables/pepper/italian-sweet-pepper-care.htm), [Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Italian_Long_Sweet_Chile_Peppers_8997.php))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Italian Sweet Pepper”** reflects both its **heritage and flavour profile**. “Italian” points to its origins in Italian kitchen gardens and Mediterranean cuisine, while “Sweet Pepper” highlights its very mild heat and fruity taste. A fun fact: in Italy, this pepper is often roasted whole on a grill or flame, then peeled and dressed in olive oil—turning the thin‑walled fruit into a rich, smoky appetizer. The broader **Corno di Toro** name literally means “Bull’s Horn,” which describes its curved, elongated shape and pays homage to the traditional Italian heirloom form. ([Seed Aholic](https://www.seedaholic.com/sweet-pepper-corno-di-toro-rosso.html))

name: Melrose
description: **Melrose Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a sweet Italian‑American heirloom frying pepper. It produces 4‑inch pods with thin walls, ripening from green to bright red and offering **virtually zero heat**. Its tender flesh and rich flavor make it perfect for sautéing, roasting or enjoying fresh in salads. The plants are early‑maturing and high‑yielding—ideal for gardens seeking a flavorful, mild pepper with Mediterranean flair. ([RareSeeds](https://www.rareseeds.com/pepper-sweet-melrose?srsltid=AfmBOor8AIgxj4FFumr7rg7f_6UYg0tizLhnRklbQ_8xL), [VegVariety Cornell](https://vegvariety.cce.cornell.edu/main/detail.php?filterBy=0&variety_id=4047))
aditionalInfo: The name “Melrose” honours the Chicago suburb of Melrose Park, Illinois, where Italian immigrants settled in the early 1900s and began cultivating this pepper after bringing seed from Italy. It gained its identity as “Melrose Pepper” because it was grown and sold locally in that community, becoming a regional staple. Fun fact: it’s known among Italian‑American gardeners for its “zero‑heat, all‑sweet” profile—often highlighted in Chicago farmers’ markets during peak season. ([Eating The World](https://eatingtheworld.net/2017/08/30/the-secret-history-of-melrose-peppers/), [Tenement Museum](https://yourstory.tenement.org/stories/melrose-peppers))

name: Purple Beauty
description: **Purple Beauty Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a sweet, mild‑flavour pepper producing compact, bushy plants with large, blocky fruits about 3‑4´ long. The pods begin green, transition to a striking violet‑purple colour, and eventually to red if left to fully mature. With crisp, thick walls and virtually no heat, they’re ideal for salads, roasting or fresh eating. ([PepperGeek](https://peppergeek.com/purple-beauty-bell-peppers/), [Rare Seeds](https://www.rareseeds.com/pepper-sweet-purple-beauty))
aditionalInfo: The name “Purple Beauty” arises from the pepper’s jewel‑like colour—especially the deep violet hue before full ripening—which makes it stand out visually in the garden. Developed in the United States by de‑hybridising the ‘Purple Belle’ hybrid, it became a sought‑after open‑pollinated variety for its ornamental and edible appeal. Fun fact: the striking purple colour disappears when cooked (turning green or red), so growers often use them raw to retain the visual impact. ([SmartGardener](https://www.smartgardener.com/plants/234-peppers-purple-beauty/overview), [Kokopelli Semences](https://kokopelli-semences.com/en/p/purple-beauty-sweet-bell-pepper-mid-season-pepper))

name: 7 Pot Bubblegum
description: **7 Pot Bubblegum Pepper** is an extremely hot chili from the *Capsicum chinense* species, developed in the UK by breeder Jon Harper ([peppergeek.com](https://peppergeek.com/7-pot-bubblegum-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The pods are small, about 2 inches (~5 cm), with a bulbous shape, reaching 850,000–1,000,000 Scoville Heat Units ([pepperscale.com](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-bubblegum/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Its flavor is fruity and slightly floral, which contrasts with the intense heat ([peppergeek.com](https://peppergeek.com/7-pot-bubblegum-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The “Bubblegum” name comes from the pink bubblegum-like color of ripe pods and the initial sweet/fruity taste before the heat kicks in ([pepperhead.com](https://pepperhead.com/shop/7-pot-bubblegum/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Interestingly, this variety exhibits “bleeding calyx,” where the color spreads to the stem as it ripens ([spicequestlabs.com](https://spicequestlabs.com/pages/the-7-pot-bubblegum-family?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The “7 Pot” label comes from the legend that one pod is enough to spice **seven pots** of stew, highlighting its extreme heat ([pepperscale.com](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-bubblegum/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: 7 Pot Primo
description: **7 Pot Primo Pepper** is a super-hot chili from the *Capsicum chinense* species, developed in Louisiana by breeder Troy Primeaux ([chilipeppermadness.com](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/7-pot-primo/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The pods are roughly 2 inches (~5 cm) long, with wrinkled skin and a distinctive “stinger” tail, reaching 800,000–1,400,000 Scoville Heat Units ([pepperscale.com](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-primo/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Its flavor is fruity and floral, providing a brief sweet taste before the intense heat kicks in ([chilipeppermadness.com](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/7-pot-primo/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The “Primo” name comes from the breeder Troy Primeaux’s nickname ([pepperscale.com](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-primo/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The “7 Pot” prefix refers to the legend that a single pod can spice **seven pots** of stew, emphasizing its extreme heat ([pepperscale.com](https://pepperscale.com/hottest-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Fun fact: its long stinger-like tail and blistered appearance give it a visual resemblance to other famous super-hots like the Carolina Reaper ([pepperhead.com](https://pepperhead.com/shop/7-pot-primo/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: 7 Pot Yellow
description: **7 Pot Yellow** is a fiery chili from the *Capsicum chinense* species, typically reaching around 1,000,000–1,200,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU) in heat. ([tyler-farms.com](https://www.tyler-farms.com/yellow-7-pot-pepper-seeds/?srsltid=AfmBOoo71fGRhRyYPRxPfFag8ZgTxESka27IJ98XwsR7PwBcg62N3R0L3)) The peppers mature to a bright yellow colour, measure similar in size to the red 7 Pot variants, and deliver a bold fruity flavour with citrus undertones before the intense heat hits. ([tyler-farms.com](https://www.tyler-farms.com/yellow-7-pot-pepper-seeds/?srsltid=AfmBOoo71fGRhRyYPRxPfFag8ZgTxESka27IJ98XwsR7PwBcg62N3R0L3))
aditionalInfo: The “Yellow” in the name simply refers to the ripe vibrant colour of the pod, distinguishing it from red, orange or chocolate 7 Pot strains. One fun fact: the “7 Pot” prefix stems from the folklore that a single pod of this lineage can spice **seven pots** of stew, underlining its extreme potency. ([tyler-farms.com](https://www.tyler-farms.com/yellow-7-pot-pepper-seeds/?srsltid=AfmBOoo71fGRhRyYPRxPfFag8ZgTxESka27IJ98XwsR7PwBcg62N3R0L3)) Additionally, growers note its deceptive tropical look—bright yellow and enticing—masks the serious heat level, making it a pepper for the experienced. ([rareseeds.com](https://www.rareseeds.com/pepper-hot-yellow-seven-pot?srsltid=AfmBOoolqQF25sXrMpd5NhMiy1))

name: Aji Cachucha
