description: **Aji Cachucha Pepper** is a mild chili from the *Capsicum chinense* species, popular across the Caribbean, especially in Cuba and Puerto Rico ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_dulce?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The pods are small and shaped like little hats, ripening from green to orange-red. They measure between 0 and 1,000 Scoville Heat Units and offer a sweet, slightly smoky, and fruity flavor, making them ideal for traditional Caribbean dishes like sofrito ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Cachucha_Peppers_14955.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Cachucha” comes from the Spanish word for “cap,” referring to its characteristic shape ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_dulce?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). A fun fact: although this pepper is mostly mild, occasional pods can surprise with higher heat due to cross-pollination or environmental stress ([Pepper Diaries](https://pepperdiaries.com/aji-cachucha-pepper-review/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Its mildness and aroma make it a favorite in Caribbean kitchens for seasoning without overwhelming spice.

name: Aji Cristal
description: **Aji Cristal Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a medium-hot chili originating from Chile, known for its bright, citrusy flavor and crisp texture ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/aji-cristal-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The pods ripen from pale green to yellow and finally to orange-red, typically reaching 20,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units. Their juicy, thin walls make them perfect for fresh salsas, ceviche, and pickling, and they’re a staple in Chilean home gardens ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Aji_Crystal_Chile_Peppers_12341.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Cristal” refers to the pepper’s translucent, glass-like skin when unripe, which gives it a distinctive crystalline appearance ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Aji_Crystal_Chile_Peppers_12341.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). A fun fact: the Aji Cristal is beloved in Chile for its use in the national condiment *pebre*, a zesty sauce served with grilled meats and bread. When green, it offers tangy brightness, while mature pods bring a fruity depth to dishes ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/aji-cristal-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Aji Dulce
description: **Ají Dulce Pepper** is a mild and aromatic chili from the *Capsicum chinense* species, widely grown throughout the Caribbean including Puerto Rico, Cuba and the Dominican Republic ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/aji-dulce/)). The pods resemble small habaneros—wrinkled, hat-shaped, and colourful—yet they deliver very little heat (typically 0–1000 SHU) and offer a sweet, fruity, and slightly smoky flavour ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Aji_Dulce_Chile_Peppers_16334.php)).
aditionalInfo: Its name means “sweet pepper” in Spanish (“ají” = pepper, “dulce” = sweet), reflecting its mild heat and gentler flavour profile compared to hotter relatives. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_dulce)). Fun fact: it is a cornerstone of Caribbean cuisine, especially used in the sofrito base in Puerto Rican and Cuban cooking, where it provides aroma and flavour without overwhelming spice. ([Epicurious](https://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/puerto-rican-style-em-aji-dulce-em-sauce-em-ajilimojili-em-51203210)).

name: Aji Fantasy
description: **Aji Fantasy Pepper** is a mild to medium chili from the *Capsicum baccatum* species, developed in Finland through about five years of selective breeding. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-heat-chili-peppers/aji-fantasy-chili-pepper/)) The pods are lantern-shaped, around 4-5 cm long and 2-3 cm wide, ripening from green to light yellow, and typically register between ~5,000 and 10,000 Scoville Heat Units. ([The Hippy Seed Company](https://www.thehippyseedcompany.com/product/aj-fantasy/)) Its flavour is fruity and slightly citrusy, with crisp thin walls that make it ideal for fresh salsas or stir fries. ([Pepper Geek](https://peppergeek.com/aji-fantasy-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Fantasy” reflects the pepper’s whimsical lantern-style pods and the breeder’s aim for an almost magical flavour experience. One fun fact: this variety is exceptionally productive — one grower reported over 100 pods on a single plant in one season. ([Pepper Geek](https://peppergeek.com/aji-fantasy-peppers/)) Also, although mild, removing the seeds and pith can reduce heat almost entirely, making it accessible for those who prefer virtually no spice. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-heat-chili-peppers/aji-fantasy-chili-pepper/))

name: Aji Habanero
description: **Ají Habanero Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a fiery chili known for both its intense heat and fruity flavour ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Habanero)). Typically measuring 2–6 cm long and taking colours from green to orange, red or even brown, it commonly registers from 100,000 to 350,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU) in standard varieties, though extreme examples go much higher. ([Pepper Geek](https://www.peppergeek.com/habanero-peppers/)) Its thin-walled pods have a distinctively floral and citrusy taste that complements its burn.
aditionalInfo: The term “Habanero” translates to “from Havana,” reflecting its association with the Cuban capital even though it likely originated in the Amazon or Yucatán region. ([Pepper Geek](https://www.peppergeek.com/habanero-peppers/)) Fun fact: despite its reputation for heat, the habanero’s flavour profile—sweet, fruity, floral—makes it a beloved ingredient in hot sauces and gourmet cooking, and certain milder bred versions retain the taste but reduce the heat drastically.

name: Aji Omnicolor
description: **Aji Omnicolor Pepper** is a vibrant chili from the *Capsicum baccatum* species, originating in Peru and ripening through shades of ivory, yellow, orange, and red, with occasional purple hues when exposed to strong sunlight. ([Pepper Scale](https://pepperscale.com/aji-omnicolor/)) The pods measure about 2–3 inches long and carry a medium‑hot heat, typically ranging from **30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units** — similar to a cayenne. ([Pepper Joe’s blog](https://pepperjoe.com/blogs/blog/aji-omnicolor-pepper-spotlight-one-of-the-tastiest-ornamental-peppers-you-can-grow)) Its flavor is described as fruity and sweet with a citrusy edge, making it both an ornamental standout and an edible delight.
aditionalInfo: The name “Omnicolor” reflects the pepper’s remarkable colour transition as fruits mature on the plant, creating a multicoloured display. ([The Hippy Seed Company](https://www.thehippyseedcompany.com/product/aji-omnicolour/)) A fun fact: despite being prized in ornamental gardens for its visual appeal, this variety delivers serious heat and is fully edible — many growers report plants producing hundreds of pods, making it ideal for hot sauces or pickling. ([Chili Craze guide](https://chilicraze.com/how-to-grow/aji-omnicolor/)) Also, the occasional purple pigmentation in early fruit stages is a plant’s response to high sunlight, adding to its ornamental charm.

name: Aji Pineapple
description: **Aji Pineapple Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a lively, medium‑heat chili variety hailing from Peru. Its pods are typically 2–3 inches long, ripening from green to a bright yellow hue, and register around 20,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU), making them ideal for those who prefer flavour over searing heat ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/aji-pineapple-chili-pepper/)). The flavour is distinctly fruity, with citrus and pineapple notes that give it both culinary versatility and visual appeal ([PepperGeek](https://peppergeek.com/aji-pineapple/)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Pineapple” comes from its tropical flavour profile—many growers describe the taste as sweet and tangy, reminiscent of pineapple, which sets it apart from many other baccatum varieties ([Pepper Geek](https://peppergeek.com/aji-pineapple/)). A fun fact: the plants are remarkably productive; one gardener reported dozens of pods on a compact plant, making it an excellent choice for hot‐sauce makers or picklers. They’re also suited to cooler climates compared to many peppers of their kind ([PepperPulse](https://www.pepperpulse.com/aji-pineapple/)).

name: Apache
description: **Apache Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a compact & prolific chili variety, producing bullet‑shaped pods about 1‑2 inches (2.5‑5 cm) long, ripening from green to bright red, and reaching heat levels of approximately 80,000‑100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU), placing it well into “extra‑hot” territory. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/apache-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) The plant is ideal for container gardening or small spaces, thanks to its bushy, dwarf habit and heavy fruiting.
aditionalInfo: The name “Apache” likely evokes the bold, fiery character of the pepper rather than a geographical origin. A fun fact: despite its compact size, the Apache pepper can deliver heat comparable to much larger varieties, making it a favourite for hot‑sauce makers and patio growers alike. It also earned the  (RHS) Award of Garden Merit for its garden‑performance and reliability.

name: Bahamian Goat
description: **Bahamian Goat Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a fiery chili from the Bahamas, producing pods roughly 1–3 inches long that ripen to a peach‑orange color ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/goat-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Its heat ranges from 100,000 to 350,000 Scoville Heat Units, offering a strong, lingering burn with tropical fruity notes like lemon and apricot ([PepperGeek](https://peppergeek.com/bahamian-goat-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The compact, prolific plants are suitable for ornamental and culinary use.
aditionalInfo: The name “Goat” may come from the pepper’s powerful “kick” or the goats that graze near its natural habitat ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/extremely-hot-peppers/)). Fun fact: the pods have a distinctive ribbed, pumpkin-like shape, making them visually striking in gardens, and a single plant can produce hundreds of pods per season, prized for hot sauces and pickling ([Encyclopedia Britannica](https://www.britannica.com/plant/pepper-plant-Capsicum)).

name: Beaver Dam
description: **Beaver Dam Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild heirloom chili from Wisconsin, USA. It produces large, thick‑walled pods about 6–9 inches long that ripen from bright green to deep red ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Beaver_Dam_Chile_Peppers_15097.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The heat is very mild, typically 500–1,000 Scoville Heat Units, with a sweet, crisp flavor, making it perfect for stuffing, roasting, or fresh slicing ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/beaver-dam-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name comes from Beaver Dam, Wisconsin, where Hungarian immigrant Joe Hussli introduced the pepper in the early 20th century ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beaver_Dam_pepper?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Fun fact: the Beaver Dam Pepper is recognized by the Slow Food Foundation’s “Ark of Taste” for preserving endangered foods ([Atlas Obscura](https://www.atlasobscura.com/foods/beaver-dam-pepper?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The town celebrates an annual pepper festival every September in its honor ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Beaver_Dam_Chile_Peppers_15097.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

name: Bell Pepper
description: **Bell Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild, sweet pepper that comes in green, yellow, orange, and red varieties. It has thick, crunchy walls and is commonly used in salads, stir-fries, roasting, and stuffing ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bell-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Unlike most chili peppers, it contains no capsaicin, making it completely sweet with a 0 SHU rating ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell_pepper?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name “bell pepper” comes from the fruit’s bell-like shape and the term “pepper” brought by European explorers from the New World ([Britannica](https://www.britannica.com/plant/bell-pepper)). Fun fact: although used as a vegetable in cooking, bell peppers are botanically classified as fruits. They were first cultivated in Mexico and Central America and gradually spread worldwide ([PepperGeek](https://www.peppergeek.com/bell-pepper/)).

name: Bird's Eye
description: **Bird’s Eye Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small yet powerful chili, typically measuring about 2–3 cm in length and ripening from green to bright red. According to [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bird%27s_eye_chili), it delivers heat levels of approximately 50,000–100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU). Widely cultivated across Southeast Asia and Africa, this slender pepper has a crisp, fruity flavour beneath its fiery kick—perfect for salsas, curries and as a condiment.
aditionalInfo: The name “Bird’s Eye” originates from the observation that wild birds readily eat the small pods and disperse the seeds, hence “birds’ eye” referring to their size and bird‑mediated spread. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bird%27s_eye_chili)) Fun fact: in Thai cuisine the Bird’s Eye is indispensable in the spicy salad *som tam*, and in African countries it’s often sold dried and pounded into a coarse hot‑spice powder. Its global journey began after the Columbian Exchange, when chili peppers from the Americas were spread by European traders to Asia and Africa.

name: Chilaca
description: **Aji Chilaca Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a long, slender chili native to Mexico, with pods typically 15–23 cm (6–9 inches) long and slightly wrinkled, dark green turning brown‑black when mature. It is mild in heat, usually ranging from about 1,000 to 2,500 Scoville Heat Units. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/chilaca-chili-peppers/)) The flavour is rich, earthy and slightly fruity, making it excellent for roasting, salsas or drying.
aditionalInfo: The name “chilaca” comes from the Nahuatl word *chilatl*, simply meaning “chili,” though this variety is often better known in its dried form as the Pasilla pepper or “little raisin” because of its dark, raisin‑like skin when dried. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pasilla)) A fun fact: fresh chilacas are relatively uncommon in markets, as most are dried to make pasillas—used widely in Mexican sauces such as mole and enchilada preparations. ([TasteAtlas](https://www.tasteatlas.com/chilaca-pepper)) Their long, shyly abbreviated use gives them an air of stealth spice—mild yet flavour‑rich.

name: Chiltepin
description: **Chiltepin Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum var. glabriusculum*) is a tiny wild chili native to northern Mexico and the U.S. Southwest. On average it reaches between ~50,000 and ~100,000 Scoville Heat Units, though some tests show it can spike far higher. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/chiltepin-chili-peppers/) The fruits are round to oval, about ¼ to ½ inch in diameter, ripen from green to vivid red, and offer a smoky, sharply spicy flavour that quickly fades. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/chiltepin-chili-peppers/comment-page-2/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Chiltepin” comes from the Nahuatl words *chilli* (chili) and *tecpintl* (“flea”), referring to the pepper’s small size and bite. ([Wikipedia](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chiltepin)) A fun fact: the Chiltepin is considered the “mother of all chiles” because it is one of the wild progenitors of many domesticated peppers. ([Chili Pepper Madness][4]) It is also protected in some U.S. wilderness areas where it grows naturally, and its dried pods have been used traditionally as a spice and preservative for centuries. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/chiltepin-chili-peppers/comment-page-1/))

name: Chinese Five Color
description: **Chinese Five Color Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a compact and vibrant chili renowned for its tiny conical pods that transition through a spectrum of colors — typically purple, cream, yellow, orange and red — all on the same plant. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/chinese-5-color-pepper/)) These peppers register around 30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units, offering a heat comparable to cayenne, with more flavour than most ornamentals. ([Kitchengardenseeds](https://www.kitchengardenseeds.com/chile-pepper-chinese-5-color.html)) The plants usually reach about 45‑90 cm tall, making them excellent for both containers and garden beds. ([HerbGarden](https://www.herbgarden.co.za/mountainherb/herbinfo.php?id=424))
aditionalInfo: The name “Five Color” comes from the remarkable ripening pattern of the pods, which display up to five distinct hues — creating a rainbow effect in the garden. One fun fact: although it’s often grown for ornamental value thanks to its brilliant colouring, it’s fully edible and makes for lively pickles or salsa with a moderate kick. The foliage itself often has a purplish blush and the contrast of fruit colours makes it popular in edible landscaping. ([RareSeeds](https://www.rareseeds.com/pepper-hot-chinese-5-color?srsltid=AfmBOoqDNs8i-Mw0NRY9J_ga6qKTM91gp0PCAM12FP9QpIeJZBLzi8pZ))

name: Chocolate Habanero
description: **Chocolate Habanero Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a strikingly hot chili cultivar known for its deep brown pods that often transition through orange-brown as they ripen. It typically scores between 425,000 and 577,000 Scoville Heat Units, making it significantly hotter than most standard habaneros. The flavor is rich and complex, with noted smoky and fruity undertones beneath the intense heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/chocolate-habanero/))
aditionalInfo: Its name comes from the chocolate-like hue of the mature pods rather than any cocoa flavor. In fact, the “chocolate” descriptor highlights its dark appearance and rich color. A fun fact: this variety is also called “Black Congo” or “Cuban Habanero,” and is prized both for its bold flavour profile and impressive heat — it’s especially popular in hot sauces and gourmet chilli products. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chocolate_habanero))

name: Corbaci
description: **Corbaci Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender and elongated heirloom sweet pepper from Turkey, with fruits reaching up to 30 cm long and ripening from pale green to yellow and then bright red. They have a mild heat—often under 1,000 Scoville Heat Units—and a rich, sweet flavor with thin walls ideal for fresh eating, frying or drying. ([TomatoFifou](https://www.tomatofifou.com/en/produit/corbaci/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Corbaci” derives from the Turkish word *çorba* meaning “soup,” because these peppers were traditionally served alongside soups in Turkish eateries. One fun fact: despite its length and delicate shape—often curving like a scimitar—the Corbaci pepper is extremely prolific, with plants yielding high numbers of fruits and often requiring support. Often grown as an heirloom garden specialty, it’s also valued for pickling and making colorful sweet pepper powders. ([SmartGardener](https://www.smartgardener.com/plants/4143-peppers-corbaci/overview))

name: Costa Rican Sweet
description: **Costa Rican Sweet Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a pleasantly sweet, elongated variety that produces ruby‑red pods reaching about 6 inches (~15 cm) in length. The flesh is medium‑thick and very flavorful, often described as richer than standard sweet bells. ([Ball Seed](https://www.ballseed.com/PlantInfo/?phid=048300001023098)) It matures in approximately 70 days and performs well in garden beds or containers, making it ideal for salads, roasting or fresh eating. ([Reimer Seeds](https://www.reimerseeds.com/costa-rican-pepper-seeds))
aditionalInfo: The name “Costa Rican Sweet” highlights the variety’s association with Costa Rica, though it’s broadly grown in warm‑climate regions. A fun fact: despite its sweet nature, this pepper is a Marconi‑type with impressive yield and versatility—gardeners report prolific harvests of long fruits that hold color and flavour well under heat stress. ([Burpee](https://www.burpee.com/pepper-sweet-costa-rican-sweet-hybrid-prod000828.html)) Its elongated shape also makes it excellent for stuffing or grilling whole, adding a gourmet touch to home‑grown peppers.

name: Datil
description: **Datil Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a small but explosive chili, typically measuring 1–4 inches (~2.5–10 cm) in length and natively ripening to a bright yellow or golden‑orange color with a date‑like shape. Its heat ranges from about 100,000 to 300,000 Scoville Heat Units, placing it in the same fiery league as the habanero. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Datil_pepper)) The flavour is uniquely sweet and tangy beneath the intense burn, making it ideal for sauces and relishes.
aditionalInfo: The name “Datil” derives from the Spanish/Catalan word for “date” (as in the fruit), referencing the pepper’s long, slender shape and golden hue. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Datil_pepper)) Fun fact: the Datil is a culinary icon in St. Augustine, Florida, where it is celebrated in an annual festival and considered the region’s signature chili; local lore suggests it was brought to the area by Minorcan immigrants in the 18th century. ([Food & Wine](https://www.foodandwine.com/datil-pepper-8674752))

name: Devil's Tongue
description: **Devil’s Tongue Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a severely hot chili known for its twisted, tongue‑like pods that typically measure 2–3 inches (5‑7 cm) long and ripen from green to golden yellow‑orange, and sometimes red. It packs an intense punch, with a heat rating between 125,000 and 325,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU). ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/devils-tongue-pepper/)) Its flavour is fruity and citrusy beneath the fiery heat, making it a favourite for spicy sauces and daring hot‑food lovers. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-pepper-types/devils-tongue-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Devil’s Tongue” comes from the twisting, pointed shape of the pod which resembles a forked or protruding tongue—symbolically attributed to the devil, reflecting both its appearance and its fierce heat. Fun fact: this pepper reportedly originated in an Amish community in Pennsylvania in the 1990s, found growing wild among habaneros. Though a relative newcomer, it has gained a cult following among chili enthusiasts for its combination of heat, flavor and visual appeal. Thanks to its relatively thick walls it’s also used in pickling and hot‑sauce production, where it lends both colour and fire.

name: Elephant Trunk
description: **Elephant Trunk Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a long, slender chili variety originally from India, bearing fruits that grow up to 15–20 cm (6–8 in) in length with a distinctive curved shape, resembling an elephant’s trunk. It is classified as mild‑to‑medium hot, around 5,000–10,000 Scoville Heat Units according to growers’ reports. The thin‑walled pods mature from green to red, making them suitable for drying, cooking, or use in spicy condiments. ([Gardener Fandom](https://gardener.fandom.com/wiki/Capsicum_annuum_%27Elephant_trunk%27))
aditionalInfo: The name “Elephant Trunk” reflects the pepper’s uniquely curved, elongated pods that mimic the shape of an elephant’s trunk. A fun fact: although it is described as “mild-hot,” many gardeners use it like a cayenne pepper—drying the fruits into flakes or powder. Also, because of its length and curvature, some gardeners train the plants to support the heavy fruits on bamboo stakes to prevent breakage. ([Reimer Seeds](https://www.reimerseeds.com/elephants-trunk-pepper-seeds))

name: Fatalii
description: **Fatalii Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a blazing hot chili cultivar that hails from central and southern Africa, offering a citrus‑fruity flavour beneath a searing heat of 125,000–400,000 Scoville Heat Units. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fatalii)) The narrow, pointed pods are typically about 2–3 inches long and mature to a bright yellow colour; red, chocolate and white variants exist too. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/fatalii-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Fatalii” has ambiguous origins—some suggest it derives from a local African language, while others believe it references the venomous nature of the pepper’s burn. A fun fact: aside from its infamous heat, Fatalii is prized for its lemon‑lime aroma, making it a popular choice for fruit‑forward hot sauces, especially those combining pineapple or mango. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-pepper-types/fatalii-peppers/)) Additionally, growers note its very quick onset of heat (unlike the delayed sting of many habanero‑types), and its thin walls make it excellent for drying into shells or pepper flakes.

name: Habanero
description: **Habanero Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a fiery chili measuring about 2–6 cm in length and often ripening to shades of orange, red, or brown. It typically ranges between 100,000 and 350,000 Scoville Heat Units—though higher readings exist—making it vastly hotter than a jalapeño. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Habanero)) Known for its thin walls, it offers a fruity, citrusy flavor beneath a blistering heat, and is widely used in salsas and hot sauces. ([Britannica](https://www.britannica.com/plant/habanero))
aditionalInfo: The name “habanero” comes from the Spanish word meaning “from Havana” (La Habaña), referencing Cuba’s role as a trade hub for the pepper. The habanero likely originated in the Amazon basin and was later cultivated on Mexico’s Yucatán Peninsula. One fun fact: in 1999 it was briefly listed in the Guinness World Records as the world’s hottest pepper. Despite that, it’s prized not just for heat but for its floral aroma and bold flavour, pairing exceptionally well with tropical fruits like mango or pineapple.

name: Jalapeño
description: **Jalapeño Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a medium‑sized chilli, commonly around 5–9 cm long, most often harvested while green but also available ripe red. It delivers a mild to moderate heat level, typically ranging between 2,500 and 8,000 Scoville Heat Units, accompanied by a fresh, grassy flavour and crisp texture. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jalape%C3%B1o))
aditionalInfo: The name “jalapeño” comes from the Spanish suffix ‑eño meaning “from” and the city of Xalapa (also spelled Jalapa) in Veracruz, Mexico—hence meaning “from Xalapa.” ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jalape%C3%B1o)) Fun fact: when smoked and dried, jalapeños become the famous chipotle pepper; also, those little brown “stretch marks” sometimes seen on fresh jalapeños are actually indicators of higher heat! ([Simply Recipes](https://www.simplyrecipes.com/how-to-tell-if-a-jalapeno-will-be-spicy-11783512))

name: Joe's Long Cayenne
description: **Joe’s Long Cayenne Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a long‑fruiting cayenne‑type chili, producing slender pods often reaching 20 cm or more in length that mature from green to deep red. It offers a medium heat level, approximately 20,000–25,000 Scoville Heat Units, with thin walls and strong flavour—ideal for drying into flakes or strings. ([Joe’s Long Cayenne seed description](https://victoriananursery.co.uk/Chilli-Pepper-Seed-Joes-Long-Cayenne/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Joe’s Long” honours Joe Sestito, who received the variety via the Sestito family of Calabria, Italy, and later introduced it to the seed‑saving community in New York. ([Seed Savers Exchange entry](https://shop.seedsavers.org/joes-long-cayenne-pepper)) Fun fact: some growers report pods reaching over 30 cm (12 inches) in favourable conditions, making this among the longest domesticated chili varieties. Its thin walls and strong drying potential mean it’s also prized for making long ristra pepper strings and chilli flakes.

name: Madame Jeanette
description: **Madame Jeanette Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a fiery chili cultivar hailing from Suriname. The pods typically ripen to bright yellow or red‑orange, and pack a serious heat punch, ranging from about 125,000 to 325,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU). ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madame_Jeanette)) With a fruity aroma reminiscent of mango and pineapple, its flavor is tropical and bold—making it a favorite in hot sauces, stews, and Caribbean‑inspired dishes.
aditionalInfo: The origin of its name is intriguing—some say “Jeanette” refers to a legendary Surinamese woman known for her fiery spirit, paralleling the pepper’s intense heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/madame-jeanette-pepper/)) Fun fact: despite its heat, the plant is remarkably prolific and compact, making it ideal for home gardens in warm climates. In Suriname it’s a culinary staple and often added whole to dishes for flavor rather than just heat.

name: Malagueta
description: **Malagueta Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a slender, upright chili typically around 5 cm long, ripening from bright green to deep red and scoring an estimated 60,000–100,000 Scoville Heat Units, placing it in the very hot category. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malagueta_pepper)) Widely used in Portuguese‑speaking countries and parts of Africa, its flavour profile combines sharp heat with a clean, crisp pepper taste and a hint of fruity tang.
aditionalInfo: The name “Malagueta” comes from the resemblance of this chile’s heat to the African melegueta pepper (also known as “grains of paradise”), though they are botanically unrelated. Fun fact: in Brazil, smaller fruits of the same plant are called “malaguetinha,” and in Portugal and Mozambique the term “piri‑piri” is sometimes used for similar cultivars—underscoring how this pepper has woven itself into a range of global spice cultures. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malagueta_pepper))

name: Piri-Piri
description: **Piri‑Piri Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a small yet intensely hot chili, typically around 2–3 cm long, ripening from green to vivid red, and commonly rated between 50,000 and 175,000 Scoville Heat Units. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piri_piri)) It offers a sharp, straight‑to‑the‑point heat with a clean pepper flavour and thin flesh, making it ideal for drying, ground spice blends or bold hot sauces.
aditionalInfo: The name “piri‑piri” comes from the Swahili word *pili pili* meaning “pepper,” which Portuguese explorers adopted as they spread the pepper into Africa and on to Europe. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piri_piri)) Fun fact: this pepper is the key ingredient in the classic Portuguese dish “frango piri‑piri,” and its spread throughout the former Portuguese colonies helped turn it into a global hot‑sauce staple.

name: Jindungo
description: **Jindungo Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a compact, potent chili variety native to Angola and widely used in Portuguese‑speaking regions. Its tiny pods, roughly 1–3 cm in length, ripen to vivid red and pack intense heat—often exceeding 50,000 Scoville Heat Units. ([DHotSeeds](https://dhotseeds.com/en/more-hot-peppers/48-jindungo-10-seeds.html)) Known for its clean, fiery flavour and generous seed count, it’s ideal for drying and grinding into spicy condiments.
aditionalInfo: The name “Jindungo” is a regional term in Angola used for what’s also called “piri‑piri” or “malagueta” in other lusophone countries. ([Wikipedia Pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pimenta-malagueta)) One fun fact: the pepper is a culinary staple in Angolan cuisine, traditionally used fresh in stews like muamba and served as a fiery sauce‑base. It is reputed to have medicinal qualities, including aiding digestion and circulation, and some local sources suggest that regular consumption may even provide mild analgesic benefits.

name: Maule's Red Hot
description: **Maule’s Red Hot Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an American heirloom chili introduced by the William Maule Seed Company of Philadelphia in the early 1900s. ([Seedways](https://www.seedways.org/product-page/maule-s-red-hot-pepper)) The plant produces long, slender pods—typically 10–12 inches (25–30 cm) in length—that mature from green to bright red. With a heat range around 15,000 to 50,000 SHU, it offers moderate spiciness, crisp flesh, and a strong drying potential for flakes or powders. ([MyGardenLife](https://mygardenlife.com/luurtsema/chili-pepper-maules-red-hot-capsicum-annuum))
aditionalInfo: The name “Maule’s Red Hot” honours William Henry Maule, a prominent seedsman whose Philadelphia-based company introduced the variety. ([MyGardenLife](https://mygardenlife.com/luurtsema/chili-pepper-maules-red-hot-capsicum-annuum)) A fun fact: despite its bold pod size, Maule’s Red Hot thrives in cooler climates and is known for its prolific yields—one gardener reported over 200 fruits on a single plant in one season. The pepper is especially valued for hot sauces, drying into flakes, or pickling thanks to its thin walls and vibrant red colour. ([The Hippy Seed Company](https://thehippyseedcompany.com/product/maules-red-hot/))

name: Mulato
description: **Mulato Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a dried‑form variety of the poblano pepper, recognized for its wide, flat‑tapering pods that measure about 10 cm long and 5 cm wide. They mature to a dark brownish‑black colour and register a mild heat of approximately 2,500 to 3,000 Scoville Heat Units. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mulato_pepper)) The flavour has chocolate and licorice notes, with subtle hints of cherry and tobacco — making it ideal for rich sauces and complex dishes. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/mulato-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Mulato” reflects the dark colour of the dried pod, analogous to the mulatto term historically used for mixed hues, and distinguishes it from the red‑ripe ancho pepper. It is a staple in Mexican mole sauces, where its mild heat and deep flavour enhance savoury ingredients without overwhelming them. ([Pepperscale](https://pepperscale.com/mulato-pepper/)) Fun fact: despite its mild spiciness, the mulato is prized for imparting smoky, sweet richness — its inclusion is often considered a gourmet touch in traditional Mexican cuisine.

name: NuMex Heritage Big Jim
description: **NuMex Heritage Big Jim Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a refined version of the legendary “Big Jim” New Mexico chile, developed by New Mexico State University (NMSU) to restore the original flavor and heat of the heirloom pod. ([NMSU CPI](https://cpi.nmsu.edu/_assets/documents/HBJ.full_.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) The plants produce large green pods—typically around 15–20 cm (6–8 inches) long with thick walls—and they deliver crisp, mild warmth and rich “Hatch” chile flavor ideal for roasting and stuffing. ([Reimer Seeds](https://www.reimerseeds.com/numex-heritage-big-jim-pepper-seeds?utm_source=chatgpt.com))
aditionalInfo: The “Heritage” name recognizes both the cultivar’s open‑pollinated origin and the selection effort to preserve its parentage. Fun facts: the original Big Jim once held a world-record for length, the new Heritage strain offers about a 10 % higher yield and five times more flavor compounds than the commercial strain. ([NMSU CPI](https://cpi.nmsu.edu/_assets/documents/HBJ.full_.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) It remains a favorite among New Mexico home‑growers and market growers for authentic flavor, large size, and versatility in dishes like chile rellenos.

name: Orange Thai
description: **Orange Thai Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) features slender pods that ripen from green to vibrant orange, each about 5–7 cm long and upright on the plant. It delivers a sharp hot flavour, commonly rated around 30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units, making it hotter than typical sweet peppers but more manageable than many super‑hots. ([Specialty Produce](https://www.specialtyproduce.com/produce/Orange_Thai_Chile_Peppers_12845.php)) Its compact plant habit and abundant yield make it popular for both kitchen gardens and container cultivation.
aditionalInfo: The name “Thai” reflects the pepper’s origins in Thailand’s rich chilli‑pepper heritage, while “Orange” distinguishes it from the more common red or green Thai varieties. One fun fact: despite its intense heat, it retains a bright, fruity flavour that enhances stir‑fries, pickles, and hot sauces. The orange colour also makes it visually striking in the garden and edible landscaping settings—growers often harvest both for ornament and for powerful cooking punch.

name: Padron
description: **Padrón Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, slender chili native to the municipality of Padrón in Galicia, Spain, typically harvested green while about 2 in (5 cm) long. It features mild heat of around 500 to 2,500 Scoville Heat Units, and is celebrated for its fresh, bright flavor and crisp texture. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Padr%C3%B3n_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name comes from its place of origin—Padrón. Fun facts: only a small percentage (roughly 10 %) of the peppers turn out significantly spicy, giving rise to the Galician saying “Os pementos de Padrón, uns pican e outros non” (“Padrón peppers, some are hot and some are not”). Their traditional preparation is simple: quickly fried in olive oil and sprinkled with coarse salt, making them a marquee dish in Galician tapas culture. ([Spanish Sabores](https://spanishsabores.com/best-padron-peppers-recipe-how-to-prepare-spanish-pimientos-de-padron/))

name: Poblano
description: **Poblano Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a large, heart‑shaped chili typically harvested while green and moist, often measuring 4–6 inches (10–15 cm) in length. It delivers mild heat—typically around 1,000–1,500 Scoville Heat Units—which makes it friendly for stuffing, roasting and fresh use in salsas. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poblano?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) Its thick‑walled flesh takes well to roasting, and when dried it becomes the dark, wrinkled “ancho” chile, prized for sauces.
aditionalInfo: The name “poblano” comes from the Mexican state of Puebla, where it is traditionally cultivated and widely used in regional cucina like the famous dish Chiles en Nogada. ([Wikipedia][1]) Fun fact: despite its mild reputation, occasional pods from the same plant can reach significantly hotter levels—offering gardeners a surprise kick. Additionally, its dried form, the ancho chile, plays a foundational role in many mole sauces.

name: Red Savina Habanero
description: **Red Savina Habanero** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a deeply red, wrinkled habanero cultivar bred in California to produce larger, hotter and heavier fruits—often measuring 2 inches by 1.5 inches and ripening to fiery crimson. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Savina_pepper)) With a claimed heat range of 350,000 to 577,000 Scoville Heat Units, it was once recognized as the world’s hottest chili. Its flavour is intensely fruity and citrusy beneath the aggressive roar of heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/red-savina-habanero/))
aditionalInfo: The “Savina” part of its name honours Savina Garcia, mother of breeder Frank Garcia of GNS Spices, who discovered a red mutant habanero plant in 1989 and developed the line. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/red-savina-habanero-chili-peppers/)) Fun fact: the Red Savina held the Guinness World Record as the hottest pepper from 1994 until 2006 and even now remains a legendary cultivar among chilli growers and hot‑sauce makers for its iconic status and extreme heat.

name: Rocoto
description: **Rocoto Pepper** (*Capsicum pubescens*) hails from the Andean highlands of Peru and Bolivia, producing apple‑ or pear‑shaped pods about 5–8 cm in diameter with thick, juicy walls and distinctive black seeds. The heat level ranges from approximately 30,000 to 100,000 Scoville Heat Units, making it significantly spicier than a jalapeño. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/rocoto-chili-peppers/)) Its flavour combines bold spice with a sweet, grassy undertone, making it ideal for stuffing, fresh salsas or roasted dishes. ([Specialty Produce](https://www.specialtyproduce.com/produce/Rocoto_Chile_Peppers_16993.php))
aditionalInfo: The name “rocoto” derives from the Quechua word *rukutu* (or *ruqutu*), reflecting its ancient use in Andean cultures. It is believed to have been domesticated over 5,000 years ago, making it one of the oldest cultivated pepper species. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Capsicum_pubescens)) Fun fact: Unlike most peppers, rocoto thrives in cooler, high‑altitude climates and can even tolerate light frost. Additionally, its thick‑walled pods are perfect for the Peruvian classic dish Rocoto relleno — stuffed with meat, cheese and spices.

name: Scotch Bonnet
description: **Scotch Bonnet Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a vibrant and fiery chili, commonly measuring 1–2 inches (~2.5–5 cm) across with fruits that ripen from green to yellow, orange, or red. It packs a potent heat of roughly 100,000–350,000 Scoville Heat Units and boasts a sweet, fruity flavor beneath its intense kick.([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scotch_bonnet))
aditionalInfo: Named for its resemblance to the flat, tam‑o’‑shanter style cap known as a “Scotch bonnet,” this pepper is deeply rooted in Caribbean cuisine. It’s essential to the classic Jamaican jerk spice mix and is also widely used in West African and Sri Lankan dishes. One fun fact: while most fruits from the same plant share similar appearance, their heat can vary widely—making consuming one a culinary gamble between mild warmth and fiery blaze.([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scotch_bonnet))

name: Serrano
description: **Serrano Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, vibrant chili originating from the mountainous regions of Puebla and Hidalgo in Mexico. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serrano_pepper)) Typically harvested while green but also available in red, brown, or yellow, it registers between **10,000 and 25,000 Scoville Heat Units**—hotter than a jalapeño but milder than many super‑hots. ([PepperGeek](https://peppergeek.com/serrano-peppers/)) The flavour is crisp and bright, with a sharp peppery bite that works beautifully diced in salsas, pickles or fresh tacos.
aditionalInfo: The name “serrano” derives from the Spanish word for “highland” or “mountain” (serra/sierra), referring to the elevated terrain where it was traditionally grown. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serrano_pepper)) A fun fact: each plant can produce dozens of pods—some yield 50 or more—and while most fruits deliver consistent heat, experienced eaters note that occasional outliers can surprise with a sharper punch. The pepper is hugely popular in Mexican cuisine and is now also grown for export worldwide.

name: Shishito
description: **Shishito Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a fruitful, slender chili from Japan, typically harvested while green and 2–4 inches (5–10 cm) long. It has thin walls, a wrinkled surface and a mild heat—usually between 50 and 200 Scoville Heat Units—making it approachable for snacking and cooking.([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shishito))
aditionalInfo: The name “shishito” comes from the Japanese words *shishi* (lion) and *tōgarashi* (chili pepper), referencing the pepper’s pointed, slightly curved tip that resembles a lion’s head. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/shishito-chili-peppers/)). Fun fact: about one in every ten pods gives a surprising kick, thanks to factors like sun exposure and plant stress. These peppers are often blistered in hot oil and served with sea salt as a popular appetizer in Japan and now globally.

name: Thai Dragon
description: **Thai Dragon Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a sleek, upright‑growing chili typically producing 5–7 cm long pods that ripen from green to brilliant red. The heat is fierce—commonly between 75,000 and 150,000 Scoville Heat Units—combined with a crisp, sharp flavour that makes it a favourite in Asian‑style dishes. ([KitchenGardenSeeds](https://www.kitchengardenseeds.com/thai-dragon-hot-chile-pepper.html)) The plant is also highly prolific, sometimes yielding over 200 fruit on a single specimen. ([RHS](https://www.rhs.org.uk/plants/251809/capsicum-annuum-thai-dragon/details))
aditionalInfo: The name “Thai Dragon” alludes to its roots in Thai cuisine and the fiery “dragon‑breath” intensity of its pods. One fun fact: despite the name implying a mythical beast, the cultivar is often grown as an ornamental thanks to its upright fruit and bushy habit—its vivid red peppers resemble blazing embers among dark foliage. Additionally, the thin‑walled pods dry rapidly and are ideal for grinding into chili flakes or creating vibrant sauces.

name: Cayenne
description: **Cayenne Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, tapering chili variety typically measuring 10–25 cm in length and ripening to a vibrant red. It registers around 30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units, making it a hot but approachable chili for many. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cayenne_pepper)) Its thin walls and high capsaicin content make it ideal for drying and grinding into the widely used cayenne powder, a staple spice for cuisines worldwide.
aditionalInfo: The name “cayenne” is believed to derive from the Old Tupi word *kyynha*, meaning “capsicum,” and also shares its name with the city of Cayenne in French Guiana, which may have contributed to the spice’s term. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cayenne_pepper)) Fun fact: while most cayenne peppers are red, yellow and purple variants exist too, and in the 19th century yellow types were quite common. Dried and crushed cayennes are the basis of many “red pepper” condiments, including hot sauces like the classic American favourite.
